body:
> Fix OnDemandPagedList underflow on slice end
> Also migrate towards yt-dlp structure

I don't recommend doing this. This was done so that it can be wrapped in `LazyList`. Since youtube-dl does not have the same and handles PagedList differently when iterating playlists, the original implementation might be better

body: Thanks, useful input. I doubt that any further changes (`__getitem__()`, eg) would be made here now that it works properly but making the low-level implementation a generator seems to make sense to me. Maybe my intuition about listify-ing an iterable vs iteratively appending lists is wrong, though, or warped by exposure to [SICP](https://mitpress.mit.edu/sites/default/files/sicp/full-text/book/book.html).

author: pukkandan

body:
I don't think this is the right solution. What you need to do is to make sure that `upload_date` and `release_date` are set if the necessary data is available. Then the year or release_year is part of a standard metadata field. There shouldn't be extractor-specific metadata.

The yt-dl core will set the second field if it's not extracted and the first is valid:
```
                ('timestamp', 'upload_date'),
                ('release_timestamp', 'release_date'),
                ('modified_timestamp', 'modified_date'),
```
So for `upload_date`, if that field might not be explicitly extracted, first check if `timestamp` is always extracted; if not, try to find some date value that can be transformed into a YYYYMMDD string (note that `unified_strdate()` doesn't work on just a 4 digit year); if there's no month or day information, fake it as `0101`, say.

Similarly, for `release_`xxx.

That sounds sensible:
* are there any unique features in this PR that ought to be preserved (maybe the updated test)?
* are you interested in making the back-port PR?

With a quick review I see a couple of yt-dlp-isms as well as some f-strings:

* `_match_valid_url()`.

I have a draft back-port of `traverse_obj()` in some branch or stash. The problem is to turn `nonlocal` into down-level code, as in the `jsinterp.py` back-port.

body:
As observed in https://github.com/yt-dlp/yt-dlp/issues/3776#issuecomment-1155586954 the user pages are currently redirecting to a captcha more or less whatever we do wrt cookies and UAs.

In a browser with JS disabled and UA set to `Mozilla/5.0` after clearing cookies for TT, a request to a user page gets the captcha page, and then reloading with the provided cookies opens the desired page. This doesn't happen with the extractor even with a delay between the two fetches.

body:
Instead of importing `unpad()`, pull this from yt-dlp's aes.py
```py
def unpad_pkcs7(data):
    return data[:-compat_ord(data[-1])]
```
and apply it to the decrypted bytes. Also consider pulling the rest of the Python AES decryption as an alternative to imported routines.

author: blablup

body:
Thanks, that's an oversight. I do actually get `ERROR: DRM protected (caused by ExtractorError(u'Missing deviceId in context',))`, but the check fails if the site decides to 403 instead,  which is what it did to you. In that case we can't know whether DRM is the issue so that error should be passed through, instead of the complaint about the exception not having a `message`.

Rebased patch pushed.

author: pukkandan

body:
> > Fixes #29915 and many others
> 
> ~~No it does not! All this will do is retry and fail again~~ (Not even that 🤦 )

I am not too aware how extractors work, but I believe the error happens becomes information extracted becomes outdated. By re-extracting the information again, it will be able to continue downloading where it interrupted. Did you test it?
Me, I am able to download long playlist (+100) songs from YouTube Music.
author: dmiraj

body:
I tend to agree with the earlier comment.

From https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/HTTP/Status/403:

>The HTTP 403 Forbidden response status code indicates that the server understands the request but refuses to authorize it. ... for the 403 Forbidden status code re-authenticating makes no difference. The access is permanently forbidden and tied to the application logic, such as insufficient rights to a resource.

However, the proof of the pudding is in the eating. If you had (as you claimed) 

>Covered the code with tests (note that PRs without tests will be REJECTED)

that showed this having an effect, that would be great.

Even so, the problem of HTTP 403 occurring during a playlist download may be a feature of relatively few sites and a work-around for those should be a feature of the extractor, or possibly also a site-specific downloader.

If the problem being addressed is that the download information expires before all the downloads in a playlist have been completed, there should be a difference between running the download with and without `--test` (which just downloads 10kB to check that the link is valid). Also `--playlist-start` should allow you to test whether the problem with (say) the 4th playlist item is that 3 previous items have been downloaded, or it's a problem with that item itself.

author: garoto

body:
May I suggest instead wording it as
NOTE: Youtube-DL is designed to maintain compatibility with legacy systems.
We recommend using [yt-dlp](https://github.com/yt-dlp/yt-dlp) instead, if you can.
Don't forget to move your config file, if you have one!
For a full list of differences between Youtube-DL and yt-dlp, see [Differences in default behaviour](https://github.com/yt-dlp/yt-dlp#differences-in-default-behavior) (this is essential reading)
Not convinced yet? Check out [a list of everything yt-dlp does that youtube-dl doesn't](https://github.com/yt-dlp/yt-dlp#new-features)

author: gamer191

body:
Personally, I was happy with the wording in #30568, though it could be expanded:

>The project has a fork https://github.com/yt-dlp/yt-dlp that offers a lot of extra functions but demands an up-to-date Python version.

Let's not mention the [current bug](https://github.com/yt-dlp/yt-dlp/issues/2701#issuecomment-1034334307) that only occurs with Python 3.8+.

As to these:
>  Mention of the break after the temporary takedown from GitHub

I think #27013 covers it but maybe something could be added to a new FAQ item concerning the legality of yt-dl; here I would defer to the project owner.

>  Short explanation of the change in management

I think #30568 covers it.

>  Reasons for still supporting 2.7

Should go in the FAQ, I think. Obviously the fact that 2.7 is a supported platform will continue to be in the first para of Description.

author: arkamar

body: What about (optionally, if installed on the system) an external interpreter. Thus, youtube-dl could be simple and do only the basics, but if intensive users want more features or more performance, then then could ensure that suitable tools are available to youtube-dl.
author: Lee-Carre

body:
At the moment PhantomJS, which was used by some extractors and is a potential dependency of of yt-dlp, similar to what you suggest, is unmaintained ("discontinued" is what Wikipedia says); owing to undisciplined feature creep a JS interpreter that worked last year will fail next year (for year in (2015, 2018, 2020)).

I had a look for other Python JS implementations, but didn't come up with anything that was (a) supported (b) compatible with Unlicense (c) compatible with yt-dl platforms; in fact I don't think any of those were satisfied.

So I think it's best is to stick with the "toy" interpreter and extend it when required.
author: dirkf

body:
> I think it's best is to stick with the "toy" interpreter and extend it when required.

Good points. I suppose a dependency is only a good idea when the benefit it brings exceeds the cost of using it.

Pity that something so obvious is lacking in the libre world. Reminds me of `wget`; highly depended upon, but widely neglected (though, maintained enough to be useful).

If only the interpreters of browsers were available as standalone modules.

At least with rolling-your-own, you have control without scope-creep.

Kudos for improving the ‘toy’.

> PhantomJS, which was used by some extractors and is a potential dependency of of yt-dlp, similar to what you suggest, is unmaintained ("discontinued" is what Wikipedia says)

That certainly was the case, for a while. The [source repo](/ariya/phantomjs) seems to have been revived (no longer archived), with relatively recent (mid-2020) commits.

As usual, Wikipedia is lacking in factual accuracy.

author: dirkf

body:
... Sorry for barging in :wink:, I not being a python coder :blush:, but, 
1. Does this **PR** aim to replicate "in a way" the automated `youtube-dl` releases to be found [here](https://github.com/ytdl-patched/youtube-dl/tags)?
2. I'm asking because those binaries (`youtube-dl.exe`), as much as they resemble the "older" **official releases** by the **previous team of maintainers**, they still are compiled **without native `Crypto` support**, which, though not a deal breaker in itself, necessitates the downloading of a certain type of streams (mainly **HLSe**) via another dependency, `FFmpeg`... 
3. I have verified that the latest, as it stands, **official release** of `2021.12.17` has been built with the `pycrypto-2.6.1` module: 

![ytdl](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/9669492/219989331-7e9d426c-b3bb-4b6e-a5a4-1fb6da214fd2.png)

so my hope/wish/suggestion 😉 is any **future binary release** (based on this **PR**) by the new management also contain "native" `Crypto` support... 

PS: The abandoned `pycrypto` module is sufficient for `yt-dl` purposes, however **PyPI** doesn't hold any wheels for it; the module contains C++ extensions, thus "installing it" from source requires compilation via a supported C++ compiler, compatible with the CPython version it's about to be used with...
Another option is to use the `pycryptodome` (**not** -x) module, for which **PyPI** wheels are available; but the end binary filesize will increase this way...
  
Kind regards 😃
author: Vangelis66

body:
> Another option is to use the `pycryptodome` (**not** -x) module, for which **PyPI** wheels are available; but the end binary filesize will increase this way...

As far as I remember, py2exe doesn't work well with it. Hence why `yt-dlp_min` doesn't use it
author: pukkandan

body:
While _ffmpeg_ isn't a hard requirement, so much yt-dl[p] functionality depends on it that I can't imagine not having it, except in some niche application of yt-dl.

Also, it solves the problem for the old Pythons that yt-dl is trying to support but which are not supported by `pycryptodome`. A Unix self-extracting build will use an installed Python and so find any pre-installed optional modules but I don't think that's true in the Windows version?
author: dirkf

body:
FWIW, there have been cases in the past where I wanted to fetch **geo-fenced media content** served over **HLSe** (AES-128 encrypted), and the only **geo-location circumvention means** available to me was a **SOCKS5** proxy (or a SSH tunnel). 

`youtube-dl` does carry native support for SOCKS proxies via the proxy switch:
```shell
--proxy "socks5://proxyhost:proxyport"
```
so it passes this down to **[hlsnative]** which, when `crypto` is available, can successfully fetch **HLSe over SOCKS**... 
`FFmpeg`, to my knowledge so far, still **doesn't support the SOCKS proxy scheme**, only HTTP[S]...

**PS**: It wasn't obvious to me back then, but, in the absence of `crypto` support, I could have used **proxy-chaining** (e.g. "The Web"<=> SOCKS5 proxy <=> Privoxy[HTTP] => FFmpeg), but why make things so complicated for the average, layman, user :wink:?

> but I don't think that's true in the Windows version?

Nope... The standalone Windows binaries built either by `py2exe`/`pyinstaller` come with the python interpreter itself and required extra python modules embedded: 
```
[debug] yt-dlp version 2023.02.14 [8b37c58f8] (win_x86_exe)
[debug] Python 3.7.16 (CPython x86 32bit) - Windows-Vista-6.0.6003-SP2 (OpenSSL 1.1.1t  7 Feb 2023)
[debug] exe versions: ffmpeg n5.2-dev-2245-N-109649-gab8cde6 (setts), ffprobe n5.2-dev-2245-N-109649-gab8cde6, phantomjs 2.1.1, rtmpdump 2.4
[debug] Optional libraries: Cryptodome-3.17, brotli-1.0.9, certifi-2022.12.07, mutagen-1.46.0, sqlite3-2.6.0, websockets-10.4
```
i.e. no type of "installation" is required at all...

The official response is to "Switch to [pycryptodome](https://github.com/Legrandin/pycryptodome)", as a quick fix.
> https://github.com/pycrypto/pycrypto/issues/290#issuecomment-533131496
pycryptotodome is incompatible with arm64 as of writing this comment as per below issue.

But it seems like a good quick fix to release something.

A _better_ fix would be to move to the `cryptography` library, but i am not aware of what this would imply. 
"most of the python ecosystem has moved on to using cryptography,
https://github.com/pyca/cryptography"

> https://github.com/tencentyun/cos-python-sdk-v5/issues/231

https://github.com/ytdl-org/youtube-dl/pull/30644

I would go with the _better_ fix. But that's just me. 
Wdyt?
author: center0

body:
yt-dl can't use _cryptography_ because of missing legacy platform support.

It can use the common API of _Pycrypto_ and _Pycryptodome_, relying on the user having installed the latter if suitable, or the former, or bundling the former otherwise. Though I'm not sure that _Pycrypto_ will help ARM64 since it expects Py<=3.3. If there is a known security issue in _Pycrypto_ it should be possible to fix it, though someone (maybe a Bellingcat-type open source investigator) who is really concerned about such issues should be running yt-dl in a sandbox.
author: dirkf

body:
> If there is a known security issue in _Pycrypto_ it should be possible to fix it

Debian still maintains pycrypto and have fixed the CVE-2013-7459 vulnerability ([#849495](https://www.mail-archive.com/debian-bugs-dist@lists.debian.org/msg1485063.html)).
See also the [changelog](https://metadata.ftp-master.debian.org/changelogs//main/p/python-crypto/python-crypto_2.6.1-9_changelog).

> ```
> python-crypto (2.6.1-7) unstable; urgency=high
> 
>   [ Salvatore Bonaccorso ]
>   * Throw exception when IV is used with ECB or CTR (CVE-2013-7459)
>     (Closes: #849495)
> ```

Someone should be able to build from source, I guess?
Source package available [here](https://packages.debian.org/source/buster/python-crypto).
`python-crypto_2.6.1.orig.tar.gz`
author: nicolaasjan

body: Imho, this is being unnecessarily delayed and side tracked. Getting any release out, even one without deps or even unsigned will cover *most* people's needs and should be the priority imo. Automation, signing, pycryptodome, standalone unix binaries are all useful to have, but is not *essential* to a release and can be added later incrementally.
author: pukkandan

body:
> Do you think that's wise?
> [Buffer Overflow in pycrypto ](https://github.com/advisories/GHSA-cq27-v7xp-c356).
> 
> https://nvd.nist.gov/vuln/detail/CVE-2013-7459

Well, indeed, both `pycrypto` tags `v2.6.1` and `v2.7a1` (the second was cut from `master` on 20131021 but never made it to **PyPI**) are vulnerable to that CVE...

Be that as it may, in the linked article it's stated _that_ CVE has been patched in the pycrypto `master` branch: 
https://github.com/dlitz/pycrypto/issues/176 => https://github.com/dlitz/pycrypto/commit/8dbe0dc3eea5c689d4f76b37b93fe216cf1f00d4

Additionally, the pycrypto GH repo has seen in 2022 some minor activity, after a long period of inertia, but has now been made a `Public archive`... https://github.com/dlitz/pycrypto/commit/8dbe0dc3eea5c689d4f76b37b93fe216cf1f00d4 is the very last code snapshot (with CVE-2013-7459 patched 👍 ). 

`pycrypto` suffers from another publicly disclosed CVE, [CVE-2018-6594](https://nvd.nist.gov/vuln/detail/CVE-2018-6594), and that hasn't been patched in pycrypto `master`, but a fix is public [here](https://github.com/pghmcfc/pycrypto/commit/2f6c124e127b5dd98723e7e75a9825c4ed8bd5c7); doesn't seem to affect the purposes for which the module is used under `youtube-dl` ...

The "official" Win-binaries produced by the former team of devs seem to contain only a subset of the full `pycrypto` package, namely ONLY **AES-related** stuff: 
```
Crypto 
  -- Cipher
     -- __init__.pyo
     -- _AES.pyd
     -- AES.pyo
     -- blockalgo.pyo
  -- Util
     -- __init__.pyo
     -- py3compat.pyo
  -- __init__.pyo
```
which is understandable, because "we" only need `AES-128` decrypting support; if this stuff is still vulnerable to the mentioned CVEs, I'm not sure...

> however PyPI **doesn't hold any wheels** for it;

For those interested and willing to trust third-party compiled wheels, GitHub holds: 

py3.5 wheels of v2.6.1: 
```
https://github.com/sfbahr/PyCrypto-Wheels/raw/master/pycrypto-2.6.1-cp35-none-win32.whl
https://github.com/hex-in/pycrypto-2.6.1/raw/master/dist/pycrypto-2.6.1-cp35-cp35m-win32.whl
```
py3.6 wheels of v2.6.1/v2.7a1:
```
https://github.com/hex-in/pycrypto-2.6.1/raw/master/dist/pycrypto-2.6.1-cp36-cp36m-win32.whl
https://github.com/hex-in/pycrypto/raw/master/dist/pycrypto-2.7a1-cp36-cp36m-win32.whl
```
py3.7 wheels of v2.6.1:
```
https://github.com/hex-in/pycrypto-2.6.1/raw/master/dist/pycrypto-2.6.1-cp37-cp37m-win32.whl
```
py3.8 wheels of v2.6.1:
```
https://github.com/hex-in/pycrypto-2.6.1/raw/master/dist/pycrypto-2.6.1-cp38-cp38-win32.whl
```
... and py3.4 wheels for v2.6.1 were available on `www.voidspace.org.uk`, but now that host has shut down; `web archive` link below: 
```
https://web.archive.org/web/20200627032153/http://www.voidspace.org.uk/python/pycrypto-2.6.1/pycrypto-2.6.1-cp34-none-win32.whl
```
For the reasons I described in my previous post, I'd prefer `youtube-dl.exe` binaries, when other pending issues get resolved, to be `Crypto` enabled...

@nicolaasjan: You routinely compile your own, py3.4-based, Windows builds, with `py2exe`; can you please confirm that substituting `pycrypto` for `pycryptodome-v3.9.9` results in non-functional builds (if the compilation succeeds at all, that is...) ? 
Thanks in advance 😄 ...

author: Lesmiscore

body:
@Lesmiscore 

Apologies for contacting you here/this way, but 😉 ...
... You appear to be the person behind the unofficial "nightly/daily" `youtube-dl` **automated builds** published below: 

https://github.com/ytdl-patched/youtube-dl/tags

and produced via GHA; most sadly, the `Rebase on Upstream` workflow runs have been **constantly failing for the past 23 (!) consecutive days**: 

https://github.com/ytdl-patched/youtube-dl/actions/workflows/rebase-on-upstream.yml

thus even the latest tag offered ([2023.04.28.334](https://github.com/ytdl-patched/youtube-dl/releases/tag/2023.04.28.334)) is built on [stale source code](https://github.com/ytdl-patched/youtube-dl/commit/6fece0a96b3cd8677f5c1185a57c6e21403fcb44) from **Mar 14th 2023** 😭 ...

As I'm sure you're aware by now, your kind service is currently the source for updated `yt-dl` EXEs for many **Windows** users, but in `master` at least 17 commits "happened" after Mar 14th: 

https://github.com/ytdl-org/youtube-dl/compare/6fece0a...HEAD

among which the latest "fix" for Google/YT's **throttling issue** (which, thankfully, doesn't appear to still be an "issue" with latest yt-player `0c487f05` 😜 ) ... I'm not asking for myself, mind you 😉 , I can build my own EXEs locally, but the "audience" you are targeting with your builds probably can't...

Huge thanks :heart: in advance for an eventual resolution/fix... 

Best regards 😄
author: Vangelis66

body:
I suppose this arises from the following chain of unpleasantness:
* GH withdraws Ubuntu 18.04 runners
* yt-dl test workflow is changed to skip test sets that needed it
* nightly workflow fails because GH needs a special "password" when modifying workflow.

But now seems to be running, many thanks.
author: dirkf

body:
Thanks **Lesmiscore** for your immediate action on the reported issue 🥇 ... Latest "nightly" tag as of this writing, [2023.04.29.1919](https://github.com/ytdl-patched/youtube-dl/releases/tag/2023.04.29.1919), is indeed built on 211cbfd 👍 !

However, to _futureproof_ the service, it's MHO the relevant `.yml` files should be modified to integrate some "safety" checks, so that when: 
a) a `Rebase on Upstream` workfow fails, the ensuing `Build` workflow shouldn't be run; it'd be a useless consumption of GHA resources, as the resulting artifacts would be identical, code-wise, to the ones produced in the immediately previous (successful) `Build` WF run; in addition, the repo owner should be somehow alerted about the `Rebase on Upstream` WF failure...
b) a `Rebase on Upstream` workfow succeeds, but the ensuing `Build` workflow fails (has indeed happened in the past); again, the repo owner should be made aware of such a failed scenario...

As I'm totally clueless myself about GHA, this is purely academic on my part, but I'm confident savvy people monitoring this PR would come up with applicable solutions (hopefully 😜 ) ...

**dirkf**: The https://github.com/ytdl-patched/youtube-dl repo doesn't (by design) have its issue tracker enabled; as the administrator of **this repo**, what would be the recommended place/way one should report a future "breakage" of the **unofficial** "daily" `youtube-dl` builds service?

> ... one should report a future "breakage" of the unofficial "daily" youtube-dl builds service?

Today's "daily" **youtube-dl** build, v[2023.05.26.810](https://github.com/ytdl-patched/youtube-dl/releases/tag/2023.05.26.810), is still built on d1c6c5c4d618fa950813c0c71aede34a5ac851e9 from **May 11th**, whereas `master` is currently on 2389c7cbd30813435c50848a9b276bcfe2a810db from **May 23rd** 😞 ; that's because the relevant [Rebase on Upstream](https://github.com/ytdl-patched/youtube-dl/actions/runs/5085991089) workflow run **failed**; excerpt from the corresponding log: 
```
+ git rebase upstream/master
Auto-merging youtube_dl/YoutubeDL.py
CONFLICT (content): Merge conflict in youtube_dl/YoutubeDL.py
Rebasing (1/1)
error: could not apply 3d8d94e6b... Automated Daily Builds
hint: Resolve all conflicts manually, mark them as resolved with
hint: "git add/rm <conflicted_files>", then run "git rebase --continue".
hint: You can instead skip this commit: run "git rebase --skip".
hint: To abort and get back to the state before "git rebase", run "git rebase --abort".
Could not apply 3d8d94e6b... Automated Daily Builds
Error: Process completed with exit code 1.
```
Regards.

author: nicolaasjan

body:
... Yes, after two successive **failed** `Rebase on Upstream` WFRs: 
https://github.com/ytdl-patched/youtube-dl/actions/runs/5085991089 (202305260049Z), 
https://github.com/ytdl-patched/youtube-dl/actions/runs/5096103642 (202305270052Z), 
a manually run `Build` WFR, though successive itself, 
https://github.com/ytdl-patched/youtube-dl/actions/runs/5096640809 (202305270251Z),
**didn't yield** an updated, sourcecode-wise, binary, as [2023.05.27.19419](https://github.com/ytdl-patched/youtube-dl/releases/tag/2023.05.27.19419) was still based on d1c6c5c and was identical to [2023.05.12.810](https://github.com/ytdl-patched/youtube-dl/releases/tag/2023.05.12.810) 😞 ...

It was today's "Scheduled" `Rebase on Upstream` WFR that succeeded and updated the forked repo, 
https://github.com/ytdl-patched/youtube-dl/actions/runs/5101593287 (202305280054Z), 
and the ensuing "Scheduled" `Build` WFR, 
https://github.com/ytdl-patched/youtube-dl/actions/runs/5102018388 (202305280241Z),
that finally produced the updated binary of v[2023.05.28.810](https://github.com/ytdl-patched/youtube-dl/releases/tag/2023.05.28.810) 🎉 👍 ...

Judging from past experience with reported similar breakages on the (now archived) `yt-dlp` ["daily" builds](https://github.com/ytdl-patched/yt-dlp/releases), **manual intervention** of some sort (by **Lesmiscore**) was always needed to bring "things back on track" 😉 , so thanks once again! 😄

author: dirkf

body:
You can take code directly from yt-dlp too. Other than some refactoring, the main difference b/w the implementations is the ability to pass arguments to a specific position for ffmpeg (which I implemented after youtube-dl had become stale)

Here's the documentation from yt-dlp for reference:
```
    --postprocessor-args NAME:ARGS   Give these arguments to the postprocessors.
                                     Specify the postprocessor/executable name
                                     and the arguments separated by a colon ":"
                                     to give the argument to the specified
                                     postprocessor/executable. Supported PP are:
                                     Merger, ModifyChapters, SplitChapters,
                                     ExtractAudio, VideoRemuxer, VideoConvertor,
                                     Metadata, EmbedSubtitle, EmbedThumbnail,
                                     SubtitlesConvertor, ThumbnailsConvertor,
                                     FixupStretched, FixupM4a, FixupM3u8,
                                     FixupTimestamp and FixupDuration. The
                                     supported executables are: AtomicParsley,
                                     FFmpeg and FFprobe. You can also specify
                                     "PP+EXE:ARGS" to give the arguments to the
                                     specified executable only when being used
                                     by the specified postprocessor.
                                     Additionally, for ffmpeg/ffprobe, "_i"/"_o"
                                     can be appended to the prefix optionally
                                     followed by a number to pass the argument
                                     before the specified input/output file. Eg:
                                     --ppa "Merger+ffmpeg_i1:-v quiet". You can
                                     use this option multiple times to give
                                     different arguments to different
                                     postprocessors. (Alias: --ppa)
    --downloader-args NAME:ARGS      Give these arguments to the external
                                     downloader. Specify the downloader name and
                                     the arguments separated by a colon ":". For
                                     ffmpeg, arguments can be passed to
                                     different positions using the same syntax
                                     as --postprocessor-args. You can use this
                                     option multiple times to give different
                                     arguments to different downloaders (Alias:
                                     --external-downloader-args)
```

author: dirkf

body:
There are a lot of fields in the infodict that doesn't have an equivalent `--get...` option. imo, it doesn't make sense to add such an option for each field. It would be more usefull to have a generic option to print whatever field (yt-dlp has `--print`).

Or, the user could just pluck the data from the `-j` output using something like jq. `yt-dlp URL -j |jq .manifest_url`
author: pukkandan

body:
Thanks for the PR, but I agree with @pukkandan.

I'd be happy to merge a back-port of `--print`. However as essentially the same thing can be achieved with `--get-filename -o '%(manifest_url)s'` (in this case), I would say that there are higher priorities.

Perhaps the above work-around (and/or use of _jq_, _[gron](https://github.com/tomnomnom/gron)_) should be a FAQ answer?
author: dirkf

body:
> the same thing can be achieved with --get-filename -o '%(manifest_url)s' (in this case)

`--get-filename` sanitizes the field as-if it were a filename. So won't work
author: pukkandan

body: Yes, not for `manifest_url` (or any field that needs to be reproduced exactly) in general, I suppose. All the more reason for `--print`, then.
author: dirkf

body:
Thank you both for the feedback.

I wasn't aware of the fork [yt-dlp](/yt-dlp/yt-dlp) and its capability to extract and get anything printed from the info dictionary. This is a nice feature indeed. And I can understand why you prefer a more generic approach.

Using `jq` as a work-around to filter out `manifest_url` was my "option" in the past. But that is not really always a good option and I don't want to elaborate why it's not.
Anyway, I remember situations when a simple `youtube-dl -j <url> | jq .manifest_url` would not do the work because there was no `manifest_url` at top-level but nested inside the array `requested_formats`. Then something like `youtube-dl -j <url> | jq '.requested_formats[0].manifest_url'` was needed. That was also one reason why I decided to create this PR and implement an additional option `--get-manifest-url` in much the same way as `--get-url`.
Unfortunately, I do not exactly remember those situations (i.e. from which website and under what circumstances). So I could not try but I guess that `yt-dlp --print '%(manifest_url)s' <url>` would fail as well in such a situation.
Today I spent quite some time to dive into the source code of `yt-dlp` (as it differs quite a lot from `youtube-dl`) to get an answer to this assumption and I found out that `url` is still specially handled, i.e. in the method `__forced_printings`, a local copy of `info_dict` gets a new entry with key `urls` and a value from what would be the concatenated output from `requested_formats`. So if you would use something like `--print '%(url)s'`, the value of `urls` is actually output instead of the value from a possibly (non-)existing `url` at top-level. As far as I can see, it is the only entry in `info_dict` that is handled in such a way. But `manifest_url` at least would need much the same special handling.
author: uhle

body: The processing for url/urls is to replace `--get-url`. For `manifest_url`, yt-dlp expects you to find it with an index path, if it's not at the top level.
author: dirkf

body:
Thank you for the kind gesture. I purposefully did not file a PR because my patch is not worthy of being merged into the main codebase, and never was intended to.

My patch was an attempt to provide a stopgap at a time when the idea of my feature request was met with strong resistance. Someone who seemed to be a team member argued that this feature should not exist in yt-dl, so my patch was aimed at helping users merge it locally into their systems.

Now if maintainers decide that the feature is worth adding, we should try and find someone who knows how to implement it cleanly and elegantly.

I recommend to add the feature, but to not use my patch for it.
author: mk-pmb

body:
The status of a commenter should be shown on the right-hand side of the message header.

Your patch is in line with the existing code structure. The fields in the `NUMERIC_FIELDS` list are separately documented. I don't think it would help to generate the list dynamically. That's why the code is as it is (as far as I know and without a lot of blame-seeking).

If this feature is to be implemented, the approach used in your patch seems fine. However it does need test(s) as I mentioned, and also updating `README.md`.

A proposal for refactoring the format selection code ought to be a separate PR, also possibly acquiring some other recent and proposed changes from yt-dlp.

body:
If you only want the info at the end of ALL processing, you can register a custom `PostProcessor` class instead

```py
import youtube_dl

class MyCustomPP(youtube_dl.postprocessor.PostProcessor):
    def run(self, info):
        self.to_screen('Doing stuff')
        return [], info

with youtube_dl.YoutubeDL() as ydl:
    ydl.add_post_processor(MyCustomPP())
    ydl.download('https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=BaW_jenozKc')
```

Or, if a hook is to be added, I would suggest a separate `postprocessor_hooks`. There are a couple of reasons:
1. You may want hooks at the start of PPs as well. In future, we may wish to send even actual progress updates (eg: https://github.com/yt-dlp/yt-dlp/pull/2475)
2. Most of the fields of `progress_hook` doesn't make much sense for PPs. Having a separate param would allow us to define/document new fields

btw, defining a metaclass/wrapper is a much more elegant way to "install" the hooks ([example](https://github.com/yt-dlp/yt-dlp/blob/ea5ca8e7fcf348a40e3eda70738738739123bded/yt_dlp/postprocessor/common.py#L15-L32)). Generally, wrappers are prefered for such uses. But in this case, metaclass has the advantage that it will automatically work for any custom PPs third parties may add

PS: There is also some personal reason why I'd like to see a separate hook. https://github.com/yt-dlp/yt-dlp has already implemented `postprocessor_hooks` (that works like I described above). So there is no reasonable way for yt-dlp to port this PR, making this the first place where yt-dlp's API significantly diverges from youtube-dl. This is bound to happen eventually, but I would like to push that as far back as possible

body:
I found the issue - the root cause is actually sitting in front of the keyboard.

So I can confirm that your PR works like a charm.

There was a bug in my wrapper script which took the command line arguments in a variable which was wrongly masked and therefore not correctly passed to the "-f" parameter:
```
[debug] Command-line args: ['-f bestvideo[height>=2160]+bestaudio/best[height>=2160]/bestvideo[height>=1440]+bestaudio/best[height>=1440]/bestvideo[height>=1080]+bestaudio/best[height>=1080]/bestvideo[height>=720]+bestaudio/best[height>=720] --ignore-errors --no-progress --playlist-random', '--verbose', '--download-archive', '/srv/x3/Hub/.download_archive_all', 'https://www.xvideos.com/amateur-channels/closeupfantasy#_tabVideos']
```

Changing my long argument string with your short version visually helped me a lot to find the problem - it's usually better to have everything more readable. Thanks for that. So my issue was absolutely unrelated to your PR.

Sorry for wasting your time - i am looking forward to see your changes soon in the master branch and later in the official binary.

> 
> if i wanted to try this fork, how do i install it? i can't find instructions anywhere. Does anyone have a step-by-step? Not sure where i went wrong, if it's already broken or what

I am using docker to build a custom youtube-dl docker-image based on a specific python3 base-image.

1. take python3
2. install youtube-dl via https://github.com/ytdl-org/ytdl-nightly/archive/refs/tags/2024.08.07.zip
3. copy some additions (config and ~patches) into image
(on production / rollout) :
4. copy modified extractors
5. build docker image
(while developing) :
6. on docker run link/bind modified extractors

---- BUT I suggest you just install it like this

author: EhtishamSabir

body:
Excellent; thank you. Nearly there, I think. A few further further things:

1. On the title, "'title': 'File:Die Temperaturkurve der Erde (ZDF, Terra X) 720p HD 50FPS.webm - Wikimedia Commons',"; the starting "File:" should be omitted.
2. If you are looking for the string `<div class="description mw-content-ltr de" dir="ltr" lang="de">` this won't always look like this. "ltr" signifies a left-to-right language, and "de" signifies that the description is in German. These can change. 
3. There are often multiple descriptions in multiple languages; these can all be imported as one description (I don't know if multilingual descriptions are supported by youtube-dl or not)
4. The test URL doesn't look right to me, as users will input the public URL starting "https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/"
author: JimKillock

body:
>     1. On the title, "'title': 'File:Die Temperaturkurve der Erde (ZDF, Terra X) 720p HD 50FPS.webm - Wikimedia Commons',"; the starting "File:" should be omitted.

Correct. It would have been stripped in the URL match. Is there actually a more title-y title in the page that could be used instead of the filename?
 
>     2. If you are looking for the string `<div class="description mw-content-ltr de" dir="ltr" lang="de">` this won't always look like this. "ltr" signifies a left-to-right language, and "de" signifies that the description is in German. These can change.

As commented, there is a utility function to get the text inside the first element with a certain class.

>     3. There are often multiple descriptions in multiple languages; these can all be imported as one description (I don't know if multilingual descriptions are supported by youtube-dl or not)

No, you can either get the first one, or do something cleverer to get a particular language (I'm assuming they're each in a `<div class="description... lang="xx">` element). The next easiest thing to do is get all the descriptions and join them with some separator.

There's no concept of a "metadata language" in yt-dl, although perhaps it should look at the current locale for such decisions.

body:
We say "caseless" where the whole world says "case-insensitive". According to references "caseless" means "having no case", and for words everyone except the Unicode standard uses it to means "having no grammatical case".

How about:
```md
    --match-title REGEX                  Download only matching titles (case-
                                         insensitive regex or sub-string)
    --reject-title REGEX                 Skip download for matching titles
                                         (case-insensitive regex or sub-string)
```

body:
Your test seems to be in a confused configuration (`C:\Python\Python310\lib\site-packages\yt_dlp\utils.py`, etc). Please try with yt-dl, like this:
```ShellSession
$ python -m youtube_dl -v -F 'http://91porn.com/view_video.php?viewkey=2d64733c15ca01adf8da'
[debug] System config: [u'--prefer-ffmpeg']
[debug] User config: []
[debug] Custom config: []
[debug] Command-line args: [u'-v', u'-F', u'http://91porn.com/view_video.php?viewkey=2d64733c15ca01adf8da']
[debug] Encodings: locale UTF-8, fs UTF-8, out UTF-8, pref UTF-8
[debug] youtube-dl version 2021.12.17
[debug] Git HEAD: c278f31d6
[debug] Python version 2.7.17 (CPython) - Linux-4.4.0-210-generic-i686-with-Ubuntu-16.04-xenial
[debug] exe versions: avconv 4.3, avprobe 4.3, ffmpeg 4.3, ffprobe 4.3
[debug] Proxy map: {}
[91porn] 2d64733c15ca01adf8da: Downloading webpage
[91porn] 2d64733c15ca01adf8da: Downloading m3u8 information
[info] Available formats for 2d64733c15ca01adf8da:
format code  extension  resolution note
0            mp4        unknown    
$ python3.9 -m youtube_dl -v -F 'http://91porn.com/view_video.php?viewkey=2d64733c15ca01adf8da'
[debug] System config: ['--prefer-ffmpeg']
[debug] User config: []
[debug] Custom config: []
[debug] Command-line args: ['-v', '-F', 'http://91porn.com/view_video.php?viewkey=2d64733c15ca01adf8da']
[debug] Encodings: locale UTF-8, fs utf-8, out utf-8, pref UTF-8
[debug] youtube-dl version 2021.12.17
[debug] Git HEAD: c278f31d6
[debug] Python version 3.9.12 (CPython) - Linux-4.4.0-210-generic-i686-with-glibc2.23
[debug] exe versions: avconv 4.3, avprobe 4.3, ffmpeg 4.3, ffprobe 4.3
[debug] Proxy map: {}
[91porn] 2d64733c15ca01adf8da: Downloading webpage
[91porn] 2d64733c15ca01adf8da: Downloading m3u8 information
[info] Available formats for 2d64733c15ca01adf8da:
format code  extension  resolution note
0            mp4        unknown    
$
```
The `get_elements_by_class()` function in the `yt_dlp/utils.py` being called in the log is fussier about the HTML syntax of the text passed to it (it actually tries to parse it instead of using pattern match) than the yt-dl version and fails because there's an unmatched `<font>`.

To port this PR to yt-dlp, one could copy the yt_dl function into the extractor module, or perhaps strip all `<font>` tags from the page before parsing:
`re.sub(r'(?i)(?:<font\b[^>]*?>|</font\s*>)', '', webpage)`

body: The API for extractors is basically the same for yt-dl and yt-dlp, but the behaviour of the `get_element[s]_by_..()` functions varies, as we can see here. The PR is for yt-dl. Possibly (untested) this line (70) could be changed for yt-dlp/ytdl-patched: ```py - for elt in get_elements_by_class('info', re.sub(r'\s*]*?>', '', webpage)) or []: + for elt in get_elements_by_class('info', re.sub(r'\s*]*?>', '', re.sub(r'(?i)(?:]*?>|)', '', webpage))) or []: ```

body:
I did wonder. I get just this with 4.3.2:
```
[dash @ 0x1c07fc0] Manifest too large: 3639796
```
which is similar to the problem in the memory issue reported for yt-dlp.

One could skip DASH formats for clips, or fix _ffmpeg_ (apparently [that happened](https://stackoverflow.com/questions/63435723/ffmpeg-manifest-too-large-when-downloading-youtube-video) but your _ffmpeg_ looks to be newer -- even if they can't name the versions in way that shows what release they're based on -- and fails differently), or hope that some DASH formats sent by YT are acceptable.

In your output, format 303 is being selected in yt-dl but _ffmpeg_ command is selecting 299+139 (avc1+aac) which aren't valid for webm. Is that why the error is being returned?
author: dirkf

body:
> or fix ffmpeg (apparently [that happened](https://stackoverflow.com/questions/63435723/ffmpeg-manifest-too-large-when-downloading-youtube-video) but your ffmpeg looks to be newer -

Yes, that issue has been fixed by ffmpeg

> In your output, format 303 is being selected in yt-dl but ffmpeg command is selecting 299+139 (avc1+aac) which aren't valid for webm. Is that why the error is being returned?

Right! That was indeed the issue

We should pass the correct stream using `-map` https://github.com/yt-dlp/yt-dlp/commit/6251555f1c670c66aa781bf9f29e9b5b33d90be1 https://github.com/yt-dlp/yt-dlp/commit/234416e4bf39d442e7abd036b7c59b8934a4086b. Note that the implementation will need to be slightly different for youtube-dl due to lack of https://github.com/yt-dlp/yt-dlp/commit/18e674b4f6bc738288b989d8b6a5f5698662a13b

body:
Yes. Duplicate of #30528.

If you want to write your own extractor for educational purposes, don't look there, nor at the [yt-dlp extractor](https://github.com/yt-dlp/yt-dlp/blob/master/yt_dlp/extractor/ertgr.py).

But if you want to do something useful, consider reviewing #30528 and
* comparing it with the yt-dlp extractor to identify any features that should be back-ported
* running the tests (and those from the yt-dlp extractor if different), identifying any failures and proposing appropriate fixes.

First read the developer instructions in the Readme of each project (but from the proposed code it looks like you did that already, at least for yt-dl).

You mean s/t like this, with appropriate deletions in `_PIPED_SITES`?
```
        'piped': '|'.join(r'(?:www\.)?' + x for x in _PIPED_SITES),

Or maybe the whole `https://` branch of the pattern should have `(?:www\.)?` and any domains that don't allow a `www.` prefix should be prefixed with `(?<!www\.)`? Who knows what the performance implications of these choices are?

author: Daenges

body:
The ld+json structure uses `@graph` which isn't supported yet. The `@context` from the top-level is meant to be used as a default in the child nodes but `json_ld()` currently just skips nodes with no `@context`.

Although a few tweaks to the parser fix this, I'd rather back-port the fancier yt-dlp code, which looks like it should already handle this and some other advanced structures, in due course.

Meanwhile let's just specialise `_search_json_ld()` in this extractor for now, like the attached.

[megacartoons.py.txt](https://github.com/ytdl-org/youtube-dl/files/8749419/megacartoons.py.txt)

author: dirkf

body:
## After fixing the issue #31087:

### Case: When URL is video and playlist link
#### Command Line
```
youtube-dl --flat-playlist --skip-download --yes-playlist --dump-single-json --quiet "https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=1uvSkDDACeo&list=PLW_omwEM1aic5BpJJUEyteXnpbaMdT3MJ&index=2"
```
Output:
```
{"_type": "playlist", "entries": [{"_type": "url", "ie_key": "Youtube", "id": "NzvOEKhk1UQ", "url": "NzvOEKhk1UQ", "title": "Adventures - A Himitsu | Royalty Free Music - No Copyright Music | Argofox Music", "description": null, "duration": 225.0, "view_count": null, "uploader": "Royalty Free Music - No Copyright Music"}, {"_type": "url", "ie_key": "Youtube", "id": "1uvSkDDACeo", "url": "1uvSkDDACeo", "title": "Cloud 9 - Valesco | Royalty Free Music - No Copyright Music | Argofox Music", "description": null, "duration": 259.0, "view_count": null, "uploader": "Royalty Free Music - No Copyright Music"}, {"_type": "url", "ie_key": "Youtube", "id": "Zby0Ug8b9ns", "url": "Zby0Ug8b9ns", "title": "Explorers - Hinkik | Royalty Free Music - No Copyright Music | SoundCloud Music", "description": null, "duration": 266.0, "view_count": null, "uploader": "Royalty Free Music - No Copyright Music"}], "id": "PLW_omwEM1aic5BpJJUEyteXnpbaMdT3MJ", "title": "Argofox Music Playlist | Label", "uploader": "Royalty Free Music - No Copyright Music", "uploader_id": "UCQsBfyc5eOobgCzeY8bBzFg", "uploader_url": "https://www.youtube.com/c/RoyaltyFreeMusicNoCopyrightMusic", "extractor": "youtube:tab", "webpage_url": "https://www.youtube.com/playlist?list=PLW_omwEM1aic5BpJJUEyteXnpbaMdT3MJ", "webpage_url_basename": "playlist", "extractor_key": "YoutubeTab"}
```

#### Python Script
```
import youtube_dl

ydl_opts = {
  'extract_flat': True, # --flat-playlist
  'skip_download': True, # --skip-download
  'yesplaylist': True, # --yes-playlist
  'noplaylist': False, # --no-playlist
  'dump_single_json': True, # --dump-single-json
  'quiet': True, # --quiet
}

with youtube_dl.YoutubeDL(ydl_opts) as ydl:
  obj = ydl.extract_info("https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=1uvSkDDACeo&list=PLW_omwEM1aic5BpJJUEyteXnpbaMdT3MJ&index=2", download=False)
  print(obj)
```
Output:
```
{'_type': 'playlist', 'entries': [{'_type': 'url', 'ie_key': 'Youtube', 'id': 'NzvOEKhk1UQ', 'url': 'NzvOEKhk1UQ', 'title': 'Adventures - A Himitsu | Royalty Free Music - No Copyright Music | Argofox Music', 'description': None, 'duration': 225.0, 'view_count': None, 'uploader': 'Royalty Free Music - No Copyright Music'}, {'_type': 'url', 'ie_key': 'Youtube', 'id': '1uvSkDDACeo', 'url': '1uvSkDDACeo', 'title': 'Cloud 9 - Valesco | Royalty Free Music - No Copyright Music | Argofox Music', 'description': None, 'duration': 259.0, 'view_count': None, 'uploader': 'Royalty Free Music - No Copyright Music'}, {'_type': 'url', 'ie_key': 'Youtube', 'id': 'Zby0Ug8b9ns', 'url': 'Zby0Ug8b9ns', 'title': 'Explorers - Hinkik | Royalty Free Music - No Copyright Music | SoundCloud Music', 'description': None, 'duration': 266.0, 'view_count': None, 'uploader': 'Royalty Free Music - No Copyright Music'}], 'id': 'PLW_omwEM1aic5BpJJUEyteXnpbaMdT3MJ', 'title': 'Argofox Music Playlist | Label', 'uploader': 'Royalty Free Music - No Copyright Music', 'uploader_id': 'UCQsBfyc5eOobgCzeY8bBzFg', 'uploader_url': 'https://www.youtube.com/c/RoyaltyFreeMusicNoCopyrightMusic', 'extractor': 'youtube:tab', 'webpage_url': 'https://www.youtube.com/playlist?list=PLW_omwEM1aic5BpJJUEyteXnpbaMdT3MJ', 'webpage_url_basename': 'playlist', 'extractor_key': 'YoutubeTab'}
```

#### Result
Command Line output and Python Script output are `consistent`.

author: lebdron

body: This PR can only be accepted if it works **and** if there is a valid [Site Support Request](https://github.com/ytdl-org/youtube-dl/issues/new?assignees=&labels=site-support-request&template=2_site_support_request.md) for it **and** if the PR template that was initially omitted is satisfactorily completed.
author: dirkf

body:
The issue templates are automatically generated from issue template templates! So you need to fix the original text in `.github/ISSUE_TEMPLATE_tmpl/*` as well. We can merge the changes for the actual templates as well, but they'll be overwritten in the release procedure.

I suppose one of the early devs was aiming for "First off". You could just have "First" instead.
author: dirkf

body:
> So you need to fix the original text in .github/ISSUE_TEMPLATE_tmpl/* as well. 

Please correct me if I'm missing something, but I committed the same change to the files in .github/ISSUE_TEMPLATE/ as well as the files in .github/ISSUE_TEMPLATE_tmpl/ .

Also, I think "First of all" sounds a little more formal than "First off" while meaning the same thing, which is why I chose that wording instead.
author: whrvt

body:
Sorry, you're quite right, as a simple count of files changed shows.

As to wording, the choice is between just "First" or "First of all", as you have, or just leave it out and make the bullet points a numbered list instead (and then the number "1." carries the same meaning.
```
Carefully read and work through this check list in order to prevent the most common mistakes and misuse of youtube-dl:
1. Make sure you are using the latest version of youtube-dl. Run `youtube-dl --version` and ensure your version is 2021.12.17. If it's not, see https://yt-dl.org/update on how to update. Issues with outdated version will be REJECTED.
2. Make sure that all provided video/audio/playlist URLs (if any) are alive and playable in a browser.
3. Make sure that all URLs and arguments with special characters are properly quoted or escaped as explained in http://yt-dl.org/escape.
4. Search the bugtracker for similar issues: http://yt-dl.org/search-issues. DO NOT post duplicates.
5. Finally, put x into all relevant boxes (like this [x])
-->
```
Actually, point 5 could be expanded, I think:
```
5. Finally, confirm that you did each of the above by putting x into all relevant boxes (like this [x]), or provide a good reason why not.
```
author: dirkf

author: Freed-Wu

body:
1) F-strings require Python >=3.6.0.

2) ytdl has always used (and the ex-/retired devs demanded) using single quotes for strings (personally I'm against that when they don't have a special purpose like they do in Bash or PowerShell).
author: rautamiekka

body:
> 1. F-strings require Python >=3.6.0.

So, even if `devscripts/*.py` (not `youtube-dl/*.py`) still must use Python >=3.6.0. really?

>     2. ytdl has always used (and the ex-/retired devs demanded) using single quotes for strings (personally I'm against that when they don't have a special purpose like they do in Bash or PowerShell).

However, the origin `devscripts/*.py` use double quotes, so which is right?
author: Freed-Wu

body:
If a string contains `'`, the project allows it be written with `""`; `'''...'''` is normally a bit verbose.

Please re-write the f-strings (eg, `f'f-string-content-with-{expr}'`) as either `'f-string-content-with-%s' % (expr, )`, or `'f-string-content-with-{0}'.format(expr)'`, or if `expr` is at the end of the format string and known to be a string just `'f-string-content-with-' + expr`.

I'll review this fully later. Meanwhile, what about https://github.com/ytdl-org/youtube-dl/issues/30900#issuecomment-1110973699?

I've updated this patch to act as a backport from yt-dlp. You can find it here. https://github.com/yt-dlp/yt-dlp/pull/4616

I've left out the third test for a YouTube embed because I'm unsure how to handle that task in this package. If you'd like that included, if can give me some pointers on how to implement it I'd be happy to add.
author: palewire

body:
> I've left out the third test for a YouTube embed because I'm unsure how to handle that task in this package. If you'd like that included, if can give me some pointers on how to implement it I'd be happy to add.

It's the exact same. Just return a url_result. Only difference as I remember is that you have to do `YoutubeIE.ie_key()` instead of just `YoutubeIE` for the key
author: pukkandan

body:
> > I've left out the third test for a YouTube embed because I'm unsure how to handle that task in this package. If you'd like that included, if can give me some pointers on how to implement it I'd be happy to add.
> 
> It's the exact same. Just return a url_result. Only difference as I remember is that you have to do `YoutubeIE.ie_key()` instead of just `YoutubeIE` for the key

In the third test, you need to have an `add_ie` item at the same level as the `info_dict`:
```
            'add_ie': ['Youtube'],
```
Apparently the test works without that in yt-dlp?
author: dirkf

body: Tests don't work in youtube-dl without add_ie? I haven't changed anything related to it in yt-dlp - and honestly have never understood what the purpose of that key is. It could be a change inherited via yt-dl**c**, though I don't see any obvious commits for it in the history
author: pukkandan

body:
I guess, something to do with lazy_extractors? What happened when @palewire ran the test in yt-dl? From the comment I inferred that it failed.

The `add_IE` thing (was cargo cult for me too) has two effects, I find:
* ` [{self.addIE}]` appended to the test name
* check that the added IE is `.working()`.

Apparently the default _flake8_ settings that the project uses changed in some way so that a keyword must be separated from the next token by a space, which is basically good but irritating. Otherwise GH somehow removed some spaces from the code, which seems unlikely and would be much worse.

If you update from the current master the 2 places where this caused problems are fixed.

author: palewire

body:
imo, this should not be implemented.
* Would all extractors be expected to do this?
* title != filename. We shouldn't extract wrong metadata just because it is convenient for filenames. Output template should be used to control filename length instead. This is a poorly implemented bandaid. 

As I see, changing the default outtmpl to something like `%(title).200s-%(id)s.%(ext)s` would be much better than this solution.

Or, if the `...` is important, move this code to core
author: pukkandan

body:
This extractor is unusual in that there is no title in the plain webpage, although a title and an `og:title` are set by JS. The full tweet text is extracted in the `description`. So:

>* Would all extractors be expected to do this?

No. But Twitter was the source of some of the original issues complaining about filename length errors with the default output template, so this is an opportunity to avoid those in future, without expecting users to check for a FAQ that tells them to use `%(title).100s` or whatever, and without changing the core code.

Instead of fixing the title, we could generate a warning if the title is "long", or possibly include the FAQ text in the error message if we could reliably detect an exception caused by an over-long filename (a generic "solution" in place of #29989).

Changing the default output template could be an alternative but I would be concerned that setting a sufficiently low value to avoid filename length issues would affect filenames on filesystems with no effective limit, attracting a different set of complaints. The extractor could manipulate the output template by inserting a length field into any `%(title)s` but that would weirdly affect all URLs in the download session.

>* title != filename. We shouldn't extract wrong metadata just because it is convenient for filenames. ...

I believe that the default output template was chosen on the assumption that `title` would be a short text field, for some definition of "short". Implicitly, yt-dl tries to cast all sites in the mould of YouTube, where the title is [(a) required (b) not longer than 100 characters](https://developers.google.com/youtube/v3/docs/videos#snippet), although SEO fiends recommend no more than 70.

Apparently the page title for a tweet set by Twitter has the form (in terms of the extracted fields) `{og_title}: "{clean_html(description)}"`, where `og_title`, the `og:title` meta field, is `{uploader_id} on Twitter`. We emulate that but remove ` on Twitter`, presumably because, where else would it be, given the context? A profile page has `{uploader_id} (@{uploader}) / Twitter`, but we don't have an extractor for those [^note]. 

[^note]: We do have an extractor for `twitter:card` but it redirects to an invalid URL and all the test URLs but the single `only_matching` URL give a "nothing to see here" page; even that gets 403 for the API call to list video details, as does the only similar URL known to G.

[Nitter tries to improve Twitter](https://github.com/zedeus/nitter/issues/124) by making a tweet title thus `{profile.fullname} (@{profile.username}): "{stripHtml(tweet.text)}"`, combining the two methods used by Twitter. Perhaps we should also do that, as `{uploader_id} (@{uploader}): "{clean_html(description)}"` (and stripping unwanted Twitter links from the `description`, as now).

In the browser title bar I see the Twitter title presented with `...` replacing as much of the middle of the text as is needed to make the text fit the title bar. Something like that still seems appropriate.

>Output template should be used to control filename length instead. This is a poorly implemented bandaid.

After all, yt-dl itself is just a bandaid in some sense. What would make for a *better* implementation? Surely even a harsh critic would call the current implementation at least "adequate"?
author: dirkf

body:
> What would make for a better implementation? Surely even a harsh critic would call the current implementation at least "adequate"?

I gave one suggestion above. As you pointed out, that has it's own issues too. (I still think this is a better solution though). If a proper solution was easy, I doubt this issue would have existed for so long

From a user perspective, I do agree this is better than nothing. But I also think that it'll become a headache to maintain any kind of consistency b/w extractors over time.

And I disagree that the twitter is special in this regard. There are plenty of other extractors that face this issue. [This search](https://cs.github.com/ytdl-org/youtube-dl?q=%22%27title%27%3A+%27md5%3A%22) is a quick way to get some examples

That said, since twitter is so popular, I can understand why you'd consider this technical debt to be acceptable.

And for the record, while an FAQ entry might help some users, I don't think it will reduce the number of issues opened. The kind of people who actually read the FAQ are the same people who'd look for duplicate issues.
author: pukkandan

body:
If you really want to get existential about it, I think the core problem is requiring a title for all sites. As detailed above, there's not a tidy title for a tweet analogous to a YouTube title. So there will never be tidy answer to this problem.

That said, making the title field optional seems like a can of worms that isn't worth opening either. Some kind of compromise is necessary and, as documented in the issue that prompted this pull request, the current solution is bad for users. So, IMHO, some alternative is needed. Something along the lines of what's proposed here does seem like the simplest solution. If universality is what you're after, I think enforcing a global trim on all filenames seems like the most natural extension.
author: palewire

body:
The rationale for `title` is presumably what I said above, trying to make all sites fit the YT metadata schema. yt-dlp has allowed a `None` title while continuing to require the `title` field without the sky falling.

>... If a proper solution was easy, I doubt this issue would have existed for so long

Amen to that. If we can get something like #29989 to work, that would fix the problem for all sites. Meanwhile:

>... since twitter is so popular, I can understand why you'd consider this technical debt to be acceptable.

Exactly. The Twitter title field is up for grabs: its extraction shouldn't routinely crash the program. I guess that extractor is used more than all the other possibly problematic extractors together, which are also a small proportion of the ~800 (crikey) we have.

And
>...while an FAQ entry might help some users, I don't think it will reduce the number of issues opened

Absolutely, why I mentioned putting some FAQ text in error message appears, if only we knew reliably what that would be.

(Unless you want to backport the entire storyboard handling code - which is quite a lot)

author: dirkf

body:
Please use my full port in [PR #30713](https://github.com/ytdl-org/youtube-dl/blob/d799c405010e50f75e253ace218de9c4be8744a9/youtube_dl/extractor/ceskatelevize.py#L26) as a basis. Feel free to define `variadic()` in this PR too, instead of locally within `traverse_obj()`, and to define `IDENTITY()`, `get_first()` and `join_nonempty()` as well.

The `from_iterable()` function in my code is needed because the library function used in yt-dlp isn't always available.

The `Nonlocal` thing is to simulate the `nonlocal` syntax for older Pythons. As you know, assigning to a previously undefined variable creates a variable binding in the current scope, unless the variable has been defined `global`. `nonlocal` tells Python to look for the variable on the call stack too, so that a new binding is not created when assigning to the variable. By making the variable that is to be nonlocal an instance value of an object, an assignment to the variable doesn't create a new binding, but updates the existing value.

`listish()` defines an expression used in several places so that the list of listish things can be updated with one change.

As you've done, `...` has to be replaced with `Ellipsis` as the `...` syntax is only in recent Pythons.

Finally, any `str` should be `compat_str`.

Even more finally, should we ask @pukkandan to write tests for it?

The code in PR #30713 was correct enough for that extractor, but as was commented extractors only use a subset of `traverse_obj()` functionality.

author: dirkf

body:
Thanks for your feedback.

There is no issue in the tracker for this. The problem this is trying to solve is that some players or devices (TVs) don't support HTML in SRT and just display the tags.

I don't know whether this is also an issue for the other subtitle formats.

One problem with my patch is that it confusingly only removes styles for subtitles converted from TTML. A proper solution would also add support for stripping the styling from unconverted subtitle files. However, that would expand the scope significantly to support many formats. Alternatively, only stripping from SRT could be supported.

So i think maybe it would be best to add a `srt-plain` format and add code to strip HTML styles from SRT files (which i'm willing to implement as it's mostly just a regex replace). Stripping styles from other formats could be implemented in the future if/when someone needs it.

What do you think?

body:
May I suggest test case?
High quality in terms of Yandex means lower mp3 compression and higher bitrate.
So, these are steps to prove that after this improvement downloaded file has higher quality:
1. Download some modern file
2. Run spectrum analyzer on it. For example, Audacity. Menus: Analyze -> Plot Spectrum
3. Compare maximum frequency
It is typical for higher-rate compression to cut higher-rate frequencies
So, we can tell that audio analyzed on firs image has higher compression rate that second one (see "cursor" field for edge frequency value).
![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/27429671/221369289-7158b2f9-bf8d-486a-92ab-38a742cda73c.png)
![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/27429671/221369323-ea5d348d-c1ee-4224-a66e-6fa24022ea81.png)

I think, this proof is quite sufficient.

Experiment can be reproduced on song 
https://music.yandex.ru/album/1891713/track/17190963

body:
Hm. These are shelves which have a direct link to get all the items, so you should prefer that than extracting from the horizontelListRenderer (which will only give a small subset of the actual results). This is what is currently happening.

```
yt-dlp --print url --flat-playlist https://www.youtube.com/channel/UCyGTmzFc3YzefTe8mOQ-QgA/playlists 
https://www.youtube.com/channel/UCyGTmzFc3YzefTe8mOQ-QgA/playlists?view=1&sort=dd&shelf_id=0
https://www.youtube.com/channel/UCyGTmzFc3YzefTe8mOQ-QgA/playlists?view=50&sort=dd&shelf_id=17666223384013636041
```

The issue in this case is the url for "Albums & Singles" is incorrect or broken - it leads back to the same page. Though it is really weird:

From https://www.youtube.com/channel/UCyGTmzFc3YzefTe8mOQ-QgA/playlists?view=50:
- If you click on albums and singles, it will take you to `https://www.youtube.com/c/sonataarctica/playlists` but shows the url as `https://www.youtube.com/channel/UCyGTmzFc3YzefTe8mOQ-QgA/playlists?view=50&sort=dd&shelf_id=17666223384013636041`
- if you click on albums and singles under the drop down, it will take you too the correct view under the topic channel, under the url `https://www.youtube.com/channel/UCyGTmzFc3YzefTe8mOQ-QgA/playlists?view=50&shelf_id=1766622338401363604`.  But if you reload, it will bring you back to the playlists page.

From `https://www.youtube.com/c/sonataarctica/playlists`
- clicking on albums and singles doesn't change the page, and you stay on the same channel
- clicking on albums and singles under the dropdown takes you to `https://www.youtube.com/c/sonataarctica/playlists?view=71&shelf_id=507`, which is the correct link and you can reload the page and it stays there.

I'm suspecting a YouTube bug that includes an inconsistency between the Innertube browse params and the url displayed for the page. 

(Also I notice that shelf_entries always try to extract entries from content when it has found a url. Not sure if that is intended, but could lead to duplicate entries.)

edit: yeah definitely a bug on YouTube's end. not sure if there is much we can do rn because adding this will trigger the issue outlined in the above note

author: Prototaxis

body:
Thank you all for your answers.

As you understand, my final goal here is to be able to have all the known albums
of an artist, provided with its channel url. Having horizontalListRenderer
working is only an instrumental goal here.

So, here I'm focusing a little bit more on that final goal, so that I can adapt
the pull request accordingly.

When using https://www.youtube.com/channel/UCyGTmzFc3YzefTe8mOQ-QgA/playlists,
youtubedl only extracts the so called "Created playlists" and not the "Albums &
Single". I found this out because I heard about the album "Acoustic Adventures -
Volume One" and realized it was not exported by youtube-dl.

By debugging the code, I realized that "Acoustic Adventures - Volume One" was
inside a document called horizontalListRenderer. That lead me to search why it
was not extracted and I eventually found out the part of the code that is the
subject of the PR.

If I understand correctly, @coletdjnz, you discourage the use of
horizontalListRenderer because it brings out only partial data. I had the
feeling that all the musics where listed when I tried it. Can you be more
specific about what should be missing?

Also, you point out that the UI of youtube is pretty weird and inconsistent and
suggest that we might not be able to do anything related to this issue.  I may
have to look at the code of youtube-dl to get my own feeling, but from a naive
point of view, I suppose that whatever youtube's UI does can be simulated as
well in youtube-dl. The fact that the UI is not consistent means that the code
will have to absorb this inconsistency. Thus, it looks to me like this makes the
data extraction more complicated, but not impossible, I mean theoretically.

Or maybe what you are suggesting is that the code to deal with this will be too
complicated to be worth it? As far I am concerned, I agree that I might not be
able to provide a pull request to do this on my own.

But, with a little help from guys like you that have experience, do you think it
would be possible?
author: Konubinix

body:
I spend some time with the code and playing with youtube and here is what I think
1. @coletdjnz I agree with you. It's quite a mess and it is likely not worth trying to simulate what youtube does. That is very unfortunate though...
2. when clicking on the singsle & albums link, the url followed is "https://www.youtube.com/pagead/viewthroughconversion/962985656/?backend=innertube&cname=1&cver=2_20220829_00_00&data=backend%3Dinnertube%3Bcname%3D1%3Bcver%3D2_20220829_00_00%3Bptype%3Dcview%3Btype%3Dcview%3Butuid%3D56F8c8khYkaA42xQhoS_gw&foc_id=56F8c8khYkaA42xQhoS_gw&label=followon_cvisit&ptype=cview&utuid=56F8c8khYkaA42xQhoS_gw", entailing your assumption. It sounds like it is a innertube migration stuff
3. by looking at the horizontalListRenderer, one can actually find the browse_id=UC56F8c8khYkaA42xQhoS_gw that, if you go to /channel/UC56F8c8khYkaA42xQhoS_gw, redirects to /c/sonataarctica
4. by playing with [innertube](https://pypi.org/project/innertube/), I find the same json that the one youtube-dl gets
5. looking at the content of the horizontalListRenderer, I actually found out that it was not thourough, as you suggested,

Therefore, I assume that there is not enough information in the youtubemusic url to get all the albums of a given artist, one should have to first find the appropriate channel (in this case UC56F8c8khYkaA42xQhoS_gw) before extracting the data.

As a conclusion, I suggest I close this pull request. 
To reach my final goal, which is to be able to list the albums from the youtubeMusic url, I will use an ad hoc script using innertube to find the appropriate browse_id, but that sounds to be out of the scope of youtube-dl.

author: Konubinix

body:
Implementing protoc into youtube-dl and requesting the shelf/grid directly from innertube is the cleanest solution I can think of.
https://www.youtube.com/channel/UCxwLkfMCfx2uSKO9w4iRfDQ/playlists?view=50&shelf_id=17666223384013636041
```py
{
context: {...},
browseId: "UCxwLkfMCfx2uSKO9w4iRfDQ", #channelId
params: protobuf.encode.raw({ #a concept function which encodes a base64 protobuf from a raw object
    2: "playlists", #tab
    4: 50, #view
    14: 17666223384013636041 #shelf_id
 })
}
```
The current issue I see with the url system failing to navigate to the correct playlist section is that it fails to correctly parse the long shelf_id which results in an invalid request, whereas the dropdown menu on the /playlist page doesn't rely on the url and requests the section directly from innertube, hence the correct result. By generating a raw protobuf parameter we bypass the faulty url system and reliably get the section response.

@Konubinix It's common knowledge. Most of the parameters used by innertube are base64 protobuf encoded

body:
If we're doing this, let's align it with the PhantomJS support in yt-dlp so that either package can be used. Other extractors might have a use for a JS interpreter (currently PhantomJS has a class in the OpenLoad extractor module).

There should be a function that runs a JS function and returns the result plus some way of determining which "engine" to use, based on options and defaults.

What do you think?
author: dirkf

body:
You mean to create something like WebDriverJSwrapper?
I think it is a good idea to make the parts universally usable. :)

P.S.
In my personal opinion, I would not recommend using phantomjs.

phantomjs is no longer maintained, and the executable that can be downloaded from the official website is statically linked to the 2016 webkit. In other words, the webkit vulnerabilities discovered since 2016 have not been fixed. Executing externally obtained JS is inherently dangerous, but even more so with a JS engine like this one.

Before that, phantomjs can read and write files and execute processes from JS, so the game is over when you load externally obtained JS. I don't think youtube would do such a thing, but I am not confident enough to say that all video sites would not do it.

When using selenium's webdriver, the JS engine is usually the browser the user normally uses, so there should be no increased risk. Also, even the average user is likely to update their browser, so it is always guaranteed that the latest JS engine can be used with the same level of security as the browser. For example, if a new JS syntax is defined, you do not need to consider it as long as you use the browser with webdriver.

Anyway, if you are going to use an external JS engine, you should use one that is maintained. :p
author: u-n-k-n-o-w-n

body:
The [lack of a plausible alternative](https://github.com/ytdl-org/youtube-dl/issues/30661#issuecomment-1047297990) is why we have the built-in mini-JS interpreter. In some applications there is no "browser the user normally uses".

Also mentioned [here](https://github.com/yt-dlp/yt-dlp/commit/600f09249f64f0318ddd831032b47c1a05637165#r81105697) is this more plausible https://github.com/amol-/dukpy

so I totally agree with you on that point.

> You mean to create something like WebDriverJSwrapper?

author: u-n-k-n-o-w-n

body: If we want to use an external binary, wouldn't `node` be better than selenium?
author: pukkandan

body:
@pukkandan 
With selenium, the browser's JS engine can be used simply by installing webdriver.
Most people probably use browsers as well, so there is often no need to go to the trouble of installing another JS engine.

In addition, since nodejs was originally designed for writing regular programs in JS, it does not seem like a good idea to pass externally derived JS to be executed(child_process, fs, etc...).
But I am not familiar with nodejs, so if nodejs can run as safely as the various restrictions that browsers impose on JS engines, then we would not need to consider this issue.
author: u-n-k-n-o-w-n

body:
> With selenium, the browser's JS engine can be used simply by installing webdriver.
> Most people probably use browsers as well, so there is often no need to go to the trouble of installing another JS engine.

youtube-dl is often used in servers/containers/embedded devices where a browser is not normally available. And installing chrome/firefox on a headless system is not trivial

PS: Also, ~~puppeteer~~ playwright is generally preferred over selenium nowadays
author: pukkandan

body:
> youtube-dl is often used in servers/containers/embedded devices where a browser is not normally available. And installing chrome/firefox on a headless system is not trivial

Yes, so this is only an workaround.
My goal is not to help all users, but to provide workarounds, even if only to a subset of users.

> PS: Also, puppeteer is generally preferred over selenium nowadays

Thanks, is [this](https://github.com/pyppeteer/pyppeteer) it?
I'll check it out. :)

Well, it would definitely be very good to have a timestamp. Unfortunately, Youtube's publishedTimeText just contains broad terms like "5 weeks ago" or "4 months ago". (If there is a more precise date/time elsewhere it would be great).
author: blueowl04

body:
The `publishedTimeText` can provide the highest resolution that YT offers if the video is new ("1 hour ago", eg). When each video page is actually processed there is an `upload_date`, but I believe the pTT is the only field available when the playlist is being processed.

Anyhow, converting this relative time text to a datetime is what the yt-dlp code that I mentioned does.

```
Perhaps the PR could be "ameliorated" :wink: to handle such cases? ... :smile:
