author: graphic-parse

body:
@mildsunrise 

Thanks for taking the time to address my comments 👍 😄 .
If I may, some additional replies/points/thoughts...

> I'm not sure what the youtube-dl policy is around skipping geoblocking 😅 [...] WDYT?

@dirkf will be the one to have the definitive say on this, but, judging from pre-existing IEs, I can safely say `yt-dl` has a pretty "relaxed" policy towards geolocation circumvention 😉 ... 

As it stands, several "localised" IEs have **playlist** APIs (i.e. APIs that generate a list of media-stream manifests) which are "soft-blocked" and can be "spoofed" by a simple `X-Forwarded-For` header added into the request; `yt-dl` implements that either automatically inside the IE or by user-issued cmdline options (https://github.com/ytdl-org/youtube-dl#geo-restriction); ` --geo-verification-proxy` is a much more special case where a whitelisted proxy is used to "fool" **just the playlist API.**.. 
Both scenarios above would ultimately work **only** when the stream CDNs don't geo-fence, i.e. media can be accessed `DIRECT`...

In the case or **rtveIE**, it appears that the first approach doesn't work (I tried this [id](https://www.rtve.es/play/videos/concepcion-arenal-la-visitadora-de-carceles/concepcion-arenal-visitadora-carceles/3303778/) with ` --geo-bypass-country ES` and no-go for me...). But what I think **should be included in this PR** is that the user be properly informed a geo-fenced programme has been attempted, and `yt-dl` should print a corresponding message; either the original Spanish string, an English translation, etc. ... 
E.g., when trying to fetch a geoblocked video from `BBC iPlayer`, `yt-dl` would print: 
```
youtube-dl "https://www.bbc.co.uk/programmes/m001c1qv" => 

[bbc.co.uk] m001c1qv: Downloading video page
[bbc.co.uk] m001c1qv: Downloading playlist JSON
[bbc.co.uk] m001c1qt: Downloading media selection JSON
[bbc.co.uk] m001c1qt: Downloading media selection JSON
ERROR: bbc.co.uk returned error: geolocation
```
... Some other IEs would additionally  advise the user to employ a HTTPS proxy, VPN and the like in the case of geo-blocked media content... 😜 

If you choose to tackle geoblocking within the **rtveIE**, I believe (most) MPEG-DASH/HLS/HTTPprogr links could be made "compatible" via a **search&replace pattern** in the CDN's hostname (at least according to [this](https://github.com/yt-dlp/yt-dlp/issues/1346#issuecomment-955547421)); `403 | 404` generating links could be "treated" with the "fix" and retried (not guaranteed to always work) ...

> Apparently the browser is fetching the obfuscated thumbnail from
> https://ztnr.rtve.es/ztnr/movil/thumbnail/rtveplayw/videos/5092259.png?q=v2
> and youtube-dl is fetching them from
> https://ztnr.rtve.es/ztnr/movil/thumbnail/banebdyede/videos/5092259.png?q=v2.
> 
> If I override self._manager = 'rtveplayw', I do get the 1280x720p stream (which is what the browser ends up playing):

I experimented a bit with 
https://www.rtve.es/play/videos/la-caza/caza-monteperdido-capitulo-1-deshielo/5092259/
(it's not geoblocked) in my browser and one can see the `HD` badge there, so I was indeed perplexed to find out your **rtveIE** could only get `576p` ...

> There's no 1920x1080p stream, but that's not available to play in the browser either. So **I'm gonna say those extra variants aren't made available right now.**

... Call me a disbeliever, but I'm not convinced they just aren't there... Why would then **RTVE** advertise them at all? 
Still with the same `id=5092259`, below sniffed request: 
https://api-ztnr.rtve.es/api/videos/5092259.json
will speak of 1080p/720p/576p/360p qualities:
```json
"qualities": [
          {
            "identifier": 5172997,
            "filePath": "http://ztnr.rtve.es/ztnr/res/_PROVIDER_/video/hd_full/_TOKEN_",
            "preset": "HD_FULL",
            "filesize": 2621046242,
            "type": "application/mp4",
            "duration": 4451520,
            "bitRate": 6000,
            "bitRateUnit": "bps",
            "language": "es",
            "previewPath": null,
            "height": 1080,
            "width": 1920
          },
          {
            "identifier": 5172998,
            "filePath": "http://ztnr.rtve.es/ztnr/res/_PROVIDER_/video/hd_ready/_TOKEN_",
            "preset": "HD_READY",
            "filesize": 1645932307,
            "type": "application/mp4",
            "duration": 4451520,
            "bitRate": 4000,
            "bitRateUnit": "bps",
            "language": "es",
            "previewPath": null,
            "height": 720,
            "width": 1280
          },
          {
            "identifier": 5172999,
            "filePath": "http://ztnr.rtve.es/ztnr/res/_PROVIDER_/video/hq/_TOKEN_",
            "preset": "HQ",
            "filesize": 1111238582,
            "type": "application/mp4",
            "duration": 4451520,
            "bitRate": 2500,
            "bitRateUnit": "bps",
            "language": "es",
            "previewPath": null,
            "height": 576,
            "width": 1024
          },
          {
            "identifier": 5173000,
            "filePath": "http://ztnr.rtve.es/ztnr/res/_PROVIDER_/video/high/_TOKEN_",
            "preset": "HIGH",
            "filesize": 637224260,
            "type": "application/mp4",
            "duration": 4451520,
            "bitRate": 1500,
            "bitRateUnit": "bps",
            "language": "es",
            "previewPath": "https://img2.rtve.es/imagenes/deshielo/1648625038352.jpg",
            "height": 360,
            "width": 450
          }
        ],
```
All four share a common recipe for 
`filePath="http://ztnr.rtve.es/ztnr/res/_PROVIDER_/video/_PRESET_/_TOKEN_"`
so if the extractor is able to detect one of them (either 720p or 576p), couldn't the rest be extrapolated  (assuming `_PROVIDER_` and `_TOKEN_` are shared) ? 

My gut feeling is the missing quality variants are reserved for specific devices/User-Agents, e.g. `1080p` for **connected** (Smart) **TVs**, `720p` for desktop browsers, etc. and hidden behind different "managers" (kind of how `mediaset` works in `iPlayer`) ...

> Fix subtitle support (https://github.com/ytdl-org/youtube-dl/issues/31248)

@alexanderadam has posted several similar requests (in both `yt-dl` and `yt-dlp` trackers) for RTVE subs support; AFAICT, not all programmes have them, but when they do, you can see the 
`Accesiblidad: Subtitulado`
info in the show's page... 

Subtitles are handled by the `subtitulos` API: 

https://api-ztnr.rtve.es/api/videos/5092259.json => 
```
"subtitleRef": "https://www.rtve.es/api/videos/5092259/subtitulos",
```
=> https://www.rtve.es/api/videos/5092259/subtitulos.json => 
```json
{
  "page": {
    "items": [
      {
        "src": "http://www.rtve.es/resources/vtt/0/6/1648554413860.vtt",
        "lang": "es"
      }
    ],
    "number": 1,
    "size": 1,
    "offset": 0,
    "total": 1,
    "totalPages": 1,
    "numElements": 1
  }
}
```
(my browser queries 
https://api2.rtve.es/api/videos/5092259/subtitulos.json
though...) 
=> http://www.rtve.es/resources/vtt/0/6/1648554413860.vtt

i.e. direct download link of the subs in the `WebVTT` format...

I believe `yt-dl` has an integrated routine to transform that to the more player-friendly `srt` format; the downloaded file can be transformed by a variety of dedicated tools, both CLI and GUI; e.g., `ffmpeg` will do it (provided the file properly adheres to the VTT specs, is not encrypted (HULU's case), etc.): 
```
ffmpeg -v 8 -stats -i "foo.vtt" -vn -an -c:s srt "foo.srt"
```
> I'm not missing anything?

... AIUI, RTVE also holds encrypted content behind **full-blown DRM** (probably the paid-for titles); in fact, in my browser I logged the following request: 
https://www.rtve.es/aplicaciones/rtve-srv-drm/?system=widevine&policy=subscription
"subscription" implies an RTVE account, does it not? 
Although highly unlikely, say a logged-in user provides account credentials to `yt-dl` via ` --cookies` and then attempts to fetch encrypted content (I've seen weirder things reported in the tracker); ~~in accordance with other IEs,~~ DRM'ed manifests (dash/hls) should be detected and the user notified about; @dirkf: is that, in fact, a general feature of `yt-dl` or specific to each IE?
author: Vangelis66

body:
Test case: https://github.com/ytdl-org/youtube-dl/issues/31276#issuecomment-1265311716

The extractor gets some 403s. If these are expected (eg out of region, not logged in), we could try to catch the warnings. The `expected_status` parameter isn't (currently) available in the manifest extraction methods but one approach could be like this:
```
...
    class TrappedError(ExtractorError):
        pass

    def _download_webpage_handle(self, ...):
        try:
            return super(thisIE, self)._download_webpage_handle(self, ...)
        except self._trap_dl_errors or (, ):
            raise self.TrappedError(...)

    def _extract_whatever_formats(self, manifest_url, video_id, ...):
        ...
        try:
            # trap 403 for example
            self._trap_dl_errors = [403]
            res = self._download_whatever_handle(manifest_url, video_id, ...)
        except self.TrappedError as e:
            # triage exception: ignore, report, raise, as necessary
        finally:
            self._trap_dl_errors = None
        ...
```
Or if the (eg) 403 comes with a diagnostic page, pass `self._trap_dl_errors` into the download:
```
            res = self._download_whatever_handle(manifest_url, video_id, ..., expected_status=self._trap_dl_errors)
```
Then if `res and res[1].getcode() in self._trap_dl_errors`, triage the diagnostic page (`res[0]`).
author: dirkf

body: Also you might consider #31085.
author: dirkf

body:
> ... is that, in fact, a general feature of yt-dl or specific to each IE?

In yt-dl it's conventional to ignore formats that "can't be played". Although there's no specific criterion for that, not being understood by _ffmpeg_ is probably a good heuristic. But we don't have any APIs for this. yt-dlp has a more nuanced and developed approach.
author: dirkf

body:
> not being **understood** by ffmpeg is probably a good heuristic.

Well, "understood" or not (how is that defined?), `ffmpeg` has no problem downloading a `cenc` MPD once the right command is issued 😉, e.g. : 
```
ffmpeg -v 8 -stats -i "https://cdn.bitmovin.com/content/assets/art-of-motion_drm/mpds/11331.mpd" -map v:0 -map a -c copy 11331.mp4 => 

frame=    1 fps=0.0 q=-1.0 size=       0kB time=00:00:00.00 bitrate=9600.0kbits/
frame=  101 fps=0.0 q=-1.0 size=       0kB time=00:00:04.07 bitrate=   0.1kbits/
frame=  201 fps=110 q=-1.0 size=     256kB time=00:00:08.06 bitrate= 260.1kbits/
frame=  297 fps=120 q=-1.0 size=     512kB time=00:00:12.01 bitrate= 349.2kbits/
frame=  301 fps= 96 q=-1.0 size=     512kB time=00:00:12.07 bitrate= 347.4kbits/
frame=  400 fps=103 q=-1.0 size=     768kB time=00:00:16.00 bitrate= 393.2kbits/
frame=  401 fps= 88 q=-1.0 size=     768kB time=00:00:16.06 bitrate= 391.7kbits/
frame=  497 fps= 96 q=-1.0 size=    1024kB time=00:00:20.01 bitrate= 419.2kbits/
(redacted for brevity)
frame= 4998 fps= 74 q=-1.0 size=   10752kB time=00:03:20.02 bitrate= 440.4kbits/
frame= 5001 fps= 73 q=-1.0 size=   10752kB time=00:03:20.06 bitrate= 440.3kbits/
frame= 5096 fps= 74 q=-1.0 size=   11008kB time=00:03:24.01 bitrate= 442.0kbits/
frame= 5101 fps= 73 q=-1.0 size=   11008kB time=00:03:24.07 bitrate= 441.9kbits/
frame= 5198 fps= 71 q=-1.0 size=   11264kB time=00:03:28.02 bitrate= 443.6kbits/
frame= 5201 fps= 70 q=-1.0 size=   11264kB time=00:03:28.06 bitrate= 443.5kbits/
frame= 5257 fps= 71 q=-1.0 Lsize=   11666kB time=00:03:30.30 bitrate= 454.4kbits/s speed=2.82x
```
The resultant file is **encrypted** and, thus, "unplayable"... 
(But I'm told very recent `ffmpeg` builds would also decrypt the file once the (somehow obtained) decryption key is fed via `-cenc_decryption_key` option...)
FWIW, I think "unplayable" might also be a misnomer 😉 ; the file does "play" in my media players, but its audiovisual content is garbled (ergo "_unwatchable_") 😄  ...

@mildsunrise, kindly have a look [here](https://github.com/ytdl-org/youtube-dl/issues/31085#issuecomment-1183201546) 😉 ; it's the same `id=5092259` but in the deprecated "**alacarta**" URL format... On July 13th 2022, a modded [**rtve.es:alacarta**] InfoExtractor would detect ALL available quality formats I've hinted about: 
```
[info] Available formats for 5092259:
format code  extension  resolution note
hls-1211     mp4        640x360    1211k , avc1.100.41@1013k, 25.0fps, mp4a.40.2@128k
hls-2113     mp4        1024x576   2113k , avc1.100.41@1864k, 25.0fps, mp4a.40.2@128k

body:
Hoping that you're well now and not wanting the best to be the enemy of the good, I've added to your checklist of the fixed and TODO features of the extractor. If we can update that and the status is as I expect then maybe it can be merged in roughly the current form and we can make a new meta-issue and PR(s) for outstanding items.

* [x] [yt-dlp issue](https://github.com/ytdl-org/youtube-dl/pull/31241#issuecomment-1254085837)
* [ ] Support for skipping geoblocking → I'd suggest leaving this to a separate PR
* [ ] For some videos, the browser uses the `rtveplayw` manager instead of `banebdyede`, which results in different qualities/variants being available
* [ ] ["unknown" format 2](https://github.com/ytdl-org/youtube-dl/pull/31241#issuecomment-1255621562) if different from above
* [ ] Fix subtitle support (#31248)
* [ ] [403s on some formats](https://github.com/ytdl-org/youtube-dl/pull/31241#issuecomment-1265345696)
* [ ] [Detect mp4 masquerading as mpd](https://github.com/ytdl-org/youtube-dl/pull/31241#issuecomment-1265357008)
* [ ] Skip (ignore) DRM formats; raise DRM as the cause if no non-DRM formats are found after skipping DRM (ie, not just `self._sort_formats()`)
* [ ] Detect additional formats [à la yt-dlc](https://github.com/ytdl-org/youtube-dl/pull/31241#issuecomment-1266188986)
* [ ] Review yt-dlp extractor and back-port features as appropriate

Regarding geo-blocking, yt-dl has a built-in feature to use `X-Forwarded-For` if the extractor has a list of `_GEO_COUNTRIES` and the extractor fails with the normal settings. A specific extractor may need to go further.

body:
Look at the comments for the `InfoExtractor` class in `extractor/common.py` to see which metadata items are known to yt-dl. If some metadata provided with the content seems relevant but doesn't match any of the pre-defined metadata items, one can consider returning a custom item.

In particular you actually need to extract the media URL!

Your life will be easier if you search for the "song data" which contains the desired URL:
```py
from ..utils import js_to_json
...
        song_data = self._search_regex(
            r'(?s)\bsong_data_%s\s*=\s*(\{.*?})\s*</script/b' % (video_id ),
            webpage, 'song data')
        song_data = self._parse_json(song_data, video_id, transform_source=js_to_json)
```
Then `song_data` will be a dict with entries like [those that I posted](https://github.com/ytdl-org/youtube-dl/issues/31203#issuecomment-1229179880).

You can go through the list of known metadata items and match them up, eg yt-dl `view_count` is the site's `plays` (I assume). `uploader` and its relatives should be matched up with items in the `.user` member of the JSON.

author: abhishek-1309

body:
What do you think ? If you return an unprocessed HLS URL, yt-dl defers to _ffmpeg_ because it doesn't know whether it can download it.

You can process the HLS URL from the `song_data` using the `_extract_m3u8_formats()` method. Use `entry_protocol=m3u8_native` to have yt-dl try using its internal HLS downloader before punting to _ffmpeg_.

Look at `extractor/common.py` for the method "documentation" and existing extractors for usage examples. Typically, you have a list `formats` to which you add format dicts for various renderings. When you find a manifest you add the returned formats, if any, to the list using `formats.extend(....)` -- in this case you only have one manifest so you can assign its result to `formats`. Finally you call `self._sort_formats(formats)`, which either sorts `formats` in place or raises an error if the list is empty.

author: miguelgargallo

body:
Yes, I can just about see how the original help synopsis might be confusing. Anyhow it's just wrong. The options don't have to precede the URLs and the URLs don't have to be provided if the options mean that yt-dl doesn't expect them.

_curl_, which has a somewhat similar UI, has the right formulation. `man curl`:
```
curl(1)                           curl Manual                          curl(1)

NAME
       curl - transfer a URL

SYNOPSIS
       curl [options / URLs]

...
```
And also
```
$ curl --help
Usage: curl [options...] <url>
 ...
$
```

body:
Sorry, I've been lax in coming to views on your points, but am now a bit more up to speed.

>If you plan to squash this during merge, I would recommend making at least 2 commits, one with 9201e8a, a90ebc0, 3abaaef and another with the rest

+1

>Should I add the commit(s) to a new git-blame-ignore-revs file? Depending on your merge workflow, the commit ids may change - so I want to confirm first. Or, you could always add it in a new commit after merging this

No, doing it afterwards sounds good.

>Should changes be made to the contribution guide and PR template? In other words, do we want to instruct devs to use pre-commit, or is it sufficient to rely on the CI?

If, as I infer from the next point, the style enforcement wouldn't automatically apply to a PR until merging, I'm inclined to say that we should just take that route instead (ie do whatever is necessary to apply the rules to commits in PRs). But if enforcement applies globally when a commit is made in a PR after the PR has been created, that should be enough.

Either way, it would be good to warn potential devs as to what will happen to their precious code, and perhaps that they can avoid surprises by configuring their forks in the same way.

>If you wish for the changes to be applied automatically on PRs, you can revert the workflow and use https://pre-commit.ci instead. There are ways to do this with GH workflow too, but pre-commit.ci is a better solution

Well, actually this sounds good: I guess it's between that and the https://pre-commit.ci/lite.html version?

>    When creating a release workflow (e.g. Add build workflow #30644), I suggest adding a step with pre-commit autoupdate to it

If the effect is possibly to update some of the checks that are being applied, shouldn't it be a separate step before triggering the release?
author: dirkf

body:
> If, as I infer from the next point, the style enforcement wouldn't automatically apply to a PR until merging, I'm inclined to say that we should just take that route instead (ie do whatever is necessary to apply the rules to commits in PRs). But if enforcement applies globally when a commit is made in a PR after the PR has been created, that should be enough.

If using current implementation:
* It will not run on existing PRs unless any new commit is pushed to it. Even a "merge with master" or a blank commit is sufficient.
* If the PR does not conform, the linter fails - with the [action details explaining why](https://github.com/ytdl-org/youtube-dl/actions/runs/3456139954/jobs/5768667392#step:4:98). This is similar to the current flake8 action. We **do not** automatically push the changes, the dev must do so manually

If using pre-commit.ci
* Same as above, any new commit should trigger the CI. I don't know if it has some way to run without a new push
* If the PR does not conform, corrections are automatically pushed to the branch without user intervention as long as the dev has not disabled "Allow edits ... by maintainers"

> Either way, it would be good to warn potential devs as to what will happen to their precious code, and perhaps that they can avoid surprises by configuring their forks in the same way.

Alright. I'll add something to docs

> > There are ways to do this with GH workflow too, but pre-commit.ci is a better solution

> I guess it's between that and the [pre-commit.ci/lite.html](https://pre-commit.ci/lite.html) version?

I was not aware of this and was thinking we'd have to build the necessary workflow from scratch 😅. But it looks good

> > When creating a release workflow (e.g. Add build workflow https://github.com/ytdl-org/youtube-dl/pull/30644), I suggest adding a step with pre-commit autoupdate to it

> If the effect is possibly to update some of the checks that are being applied, shouldn't it be a separate step before triggering the release?

Fair. My thought was that adding to build workflow would be easiest and predictable. But we could also add it as a new timed workflow (per week?). Or, if the automatic push to master is a concern, we could also [let it create a PR, like dependabot](https://browniebroke.com/blog/gh-action-pre-commit-autoupdate/).

Ofc, this is not relevant if using precommit.ci, since they already have a dependabot-like mechanism built in

body:
Thanks, but I doubt that this is a good solution.

The existing YT extradtor knows about a whole lot of Invidious instances. I believe that your problem is just that the list of instances in `extractor/youtube.py` doesn't include the ones you want. Creating a separate extractor with another unmaintainable list will just make it worse. Or is there some way in which the extraction in the YT module is unsatisfactory for the currently supported IV sites?

See also #29885. The discussion there is now really of historical interest, though (and also the linked PR) because yt-dlp has now implemented a page-based extraction system in the generic extractor to handle these cases (Invidious, PeerTube, etc). yt-dl will eventually pull this in instead of the original PR, so as to maximise commonality and avoid incompatible reinvention.

I've added `r'(?:www\.)?yt\.artemislena\.eu'` to `_INVIDIOUS_SITES`.
And also added `print("self._downloader.urlopen called with", url_or_request)` before [this line](https://github.com/ytdl-org/youtube-dl/blob/604762a9f8fa21de3f7349bd612c4f34941a5d20/youtube_dl/extractor/common.py#L635).
And here is the output:

$ python -m youtube_dl --verbose -F https://yt.artemislena.eu/watch\?v\=BaW_jenozKc

This behavior can be problematic in environments with limited access to youtube itself.

But even if googlevideo.com was accessible, I wouldn't be able to extract the download link in the first place.
author: OverShifted

body:
Normally a benefit of yt-dl vs using the YT web interface is to avoid the odious bloat of the latter while being able to capture a lot of the detailed metadata that comes with it.

If a user who has YT access wants yt-dl to process an Invidious page, going to YT directly can give a better result because (AFAIK) less rich metadata is available on the IV page. The [API](https://docs.invidious.io/api/) is another matter, but reproducing the details of the YT extractor using the IV API would be a massive task.

**But** if YT is blocked for the user, it would plainly be better to use the IV page instead. The problem is how to combine these tactics. For one-off uses, the IV page may have a download function, but yt-dl users are going to want a batchable solution.

And all these considerations apply equally for other YT front-ends, which seem to be proliferating.
author: dirkf

body:
IMHO, when a user gives yt-dl an invidious link, he/she probably wants to download from invidious servers.
Because otherwise, he/she could just "convert" that to a youtube link. (just replace the host with `youtube.com`)
author: OverShifted

body:
> IMHO, when a user gives yt-dl an invidious link, he/she probably wants to download from invidious servers.
Because otherwise, he/she could just "convert" that to a youtube link. (just replace the host with youtube.com)

If this were a completely new feature, I would agree. But we have be auto-translating invidious inks to youtube for a long time. This means many users would be expecting to get all the metadata youtube provides even with a invidious URL. Having the new extractor return less data is a regression. Perhaps a `invidious:` prefix could be supported similar to `teachable:`
author: pukkandan

body:
Maybe, as there are other front-end sites for which the same issue could arise, we should introduce an option like `--[no-]extract-page-only` with `no-` being the default (surely not the best option name). Then an IV extractor could check this and by default punt to `self.url_result('https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=' + video_id, ie='Youtube')`; or if `--extract-page-only` it could go ahead and extract the IV page without touching YT.

This might also apply where a site has links and metadata in the page but could also use some API URL(s) for more metadata and formats, whether to avoid blocked URLs or increase extraction speed.
author: dirkf

body:
> This means many users would be expecting to get all the metadata youtube provides even with a invidious URL.

If that were the case, why would they use an invidious url?
> Having the new extractor return less data is a regression.

I struggle to see how performing the expected behaviour is a regression. Invidious is always going to be worse than youtube, but that doesn't mean people who pass invidious urls expect their urls to be silently converted to youtube urls



> we should introduce an option like `--[no-]extract-page-only` with `no-` being the default

That seems reasonable, although I think there should be a warning if someone passes an invidious url with neither option, and people can silence that warning by explicitly using `--no-extract-page-only` (I don't know if that's actually possible to implement)



> This might also apply where a site has links and metadata in the page but could also use some API URL(s) for more metadata and formats, whether to avoid blocked URLs or increase extraction speed.

Wanting to avoid Google feels like a completely different use-case to not wanting to download from the website you're using's api.

author: krasnh

body:
> > Having the new extractor return less data is a regression.
> 
> 
> 
> If this is an issue (and imo it's not) perhaps the new invidious extractor should be limited to new instances (that aren't in youtube.py)

@gamer191 Invidious will always return less data than YouTube, regardless of which version of Invidious that you use. It also doesn't support things like multiple audio tracks and subtitle translating (the have to use the Innertube transcript API endpoint, which doesn't support translating, and convert the response to WebVTT, as the publicly listed instances get ratelimited on YouTube's subtitle endpoint). The format list `-F` would also be useless if you built it based on the Invidious API, as it returns hardcoded dimensions based on the itag (most noticeable for vertical videos, because the dimensions will be horizontal).
author: absidue

body:
Consider the two use cases:
1. I want to access YouTube content through Invidious without ever directly interacting with YT servers, because they are unreachable for me, or because I dislike them, or whatever.
2. I want to access YouTube content through Invidious because I got a link to  Invidious and actually had no idea that it was anything to do with YouTube.

Since the second case was trivial, if tiresome, to support, that's what happened.

Arguably the first case should have been given priority, since it would have supported users who need (or want) Invidious to act as a proxy, and so are content with whatever limitations that implies.
author: dirkf

body:
I do agree that passing an Invidious URL should download from Invidious, I just wanted to point out that there is significantly less usable metadata that you might have thought at fist. So you'll either have to decided to show the incorrect metadata that Invidious returns or not show it at all, in either case you are likely to get user complaints.

My point is that while the change does seem like a good idea it will be a breaking change, which you'll want to mention clearly in the changelog and potentially even log a warning message for a while.

Think of it from a users perspective if you upgrade youtube-dl and suddenly your format filter/selector no longer works, because height, width and fps are not available or completely incorrect, you would want to be clearly informed during downloading why that is happening.

body:
Feel free to **complete the description** in the PR template.

The proposed change would only be valid if there was a _sudo_ program in Windows, which there certainly wasn't when the text was written and AFAIK this is still true.

There is now a usable Windows Subsystem for Linux with a _sudo_ command. If you can demonstrate installing yt-dl release in WSL using the procedure laid out for Unixes, we might consider something like this:
```diff
 To install it right away for all UNIX users (Linux, macOS, etc.), type:

 sudo curl -L https://yt-dl.org/downloads/latest/youtube-dl -o /usr/local/bin/youtube-dl
 sudo chmod a+rx /usr/local/bin/youtube-dl

 If you do not have curl, you can alternatively use a recent wget:

 sudo wget https://yt-dl.org/downloads/latest/youtube-dl -O /usr/local/bin/youtube-dl
 sudo chmod a+rx /usr/local/bin/youtube-dl

-Windows users can download an .exe file and place it in any location on their PATH except for %SYSTEMROOT%\System32 (e.g. do not put in C:\Windows\System32).
+You can also use this procedure in Windows Subsystem for Linux. Otherwise Windows users can download an .exe file and place it in any location on their PATH except for %SYSTEMROOT%\System32 (e.g. do not put in C:\Windows\System32).
```
My guess is that the WSL installation procedure doesn't put the program on the `PATH` for ordinary Windows use but I'd be happy to receive advice from someone like the owner (@ethanhs) of [this repo](https://github.com/ethanhs/WSL-Programs) which reports "works perfectly".

author: Fuzion24

body:
Likewise. It was really a rhetorical question: the regex must have been an easy win.

Something like this makes it clearer what's being removed and what kept. It's not obvious what the right answer for (eg) `https://example.com/path1/path2` should be, vs. `https://example.com/path1/path2/`. The `url_basename()` function above gives `path2` for both, while the regex version of `base_url()` gives `https://example.com/path1/`, `https://example.com/path1/path2/`. A little specification comment would have been reassuring.
```py
def base_url(url):
    """
        Return the URL, with its path trimmed after the rightmost / 
        and without any query, params or fragment
    """
    parsed_url = compat_urlparse.urlparse(url)
    path = parsed_url.path.rsplit('/')[0]
    # don't restore trailing //: correct?
    if not path.endswith('/'):
        path += '/'
    return compat_urlparse.urlunparse(parsed_url._replace(
        path=path, query='', params='', fragment='')
```
author: dirkf

body:
Of course the "right answer" is what `test/test_utils.py` says:
```py
    def test_base_url(self):
        self.assertEqual(base_url('http://foo.de/'), 'http://foo.de/')
        self.assertEqual(base_url('http://foo.de/bar'), 'http://foo.de/')
        self.assertEqual(base_url('http://foo.de/bar/'), 'http://foo.de/bar/')
        self.assertEqual(base_url('http://foo.de/bar/baz'), 'http://foo.de/bar/')
        self.assertEqual(base_url('http://foo.de/bar/baz?x=z/x/c'), 'http://foo.de/bar/')
```
author: dirkf

body:
And also, [RFC 3986](https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc3986) 2.2 categorises `&` as a reserved character of type `sub-delims`:
```
      sub-delims  = "!" / "$" / "&" / "'" / "(" / ")"
                  / "*" / "+" / "," / ";" / "="
```
The principle is that a `*sub*-delims` character like `&` need only be percent-encoded if it appears in a context where it has a delimiting role. Thus in `http://example.com/contains_an_&_here?param=contains_an_&_there`, the here `&` is fine but the there `&` must be rendered as `%26`, `http://example.com/contains_an_&_here?param=contains_an_%26_there`:
```
   URI producing applications should percent-encode data octets that
   correspond to characters in the reserved set unless these characters
   are specifically allowed by the URI scheme to represent data in that
   component.  If a reserved character is found in a URI component and
   no delimiting role is known for that character, then it must be
   interpreted as representing the data octet corresponding to that
   character's encoding in US-ASCII.
```
In other words, `base_url()` *should* support `&` in the path part of a URL.

author: jamilkarami

body:
Tried to test using this video: https://kick.com/video/e466d965-b67d-471d-bed5-e3d4f19a4a9d but unfortunately it returned HTTP 403: forbidden error. The extractor is not working for me.

I've had a look at https://github.com/yt-dlp/yt-dlp/issues/6748 and it seems to be an issue with cloudflare fingerprinting. I've tried the solutions offered like using my browser's user-agent string: `python -m youtube_dl -v --user-agent ''USER AGENT'' https://kick.com/video/56292fc4-98fe-4fc8-8267-8082651bde12` but unfortunately it did not work for me. I've tried passing cookies from browser but there is no `--cookies-from-browser` option in youtube-dl (a new feature for the future maybe?) but no success it still returns HTTP 403: forbidden error.
author: aaron-tan

body: No, thanks.
author: dirkf

body: As the linked issue didn't use the Site Support template, please complete the template for this site, posted either here, or in the issue, or in a new issue.
author: dirkf

body:
Hi @dirkf, 
Sorry, I dont understand what I have to do. Perhaps my limited knowledge of english. Where can I finde the "Site Support Template"? I filled out the above form for the  pull request and yes, I forgot to write something in the description, but there is not much to describe. It is an extractor for erocast.me 

And the linked Issue: https://github.com/ytdl-org/youtube-dl/issues/31203 contains the requested issue form. 

Please tell me what to do.

Thanks,
  Ronni
author: RonniCGN

body: Sorry, the template *was* used, so ignore that.
author: dirkf

body: I'm sad this seemingly got stuck, I found an audio file on Erocast recently and the site is not particularly screen-reader friendly. I know enough to get the audio file out using developer tools but not enough to write code to automate it based on a URL. By this point I'd guess the original PR author isn't working on it anymore.
author: Simon818

body:
Hi all,

I've had a look at the code by the original author and I've noticed there wasn't any functionality to allow downloads of playlists from the site. I decided to have a look over the last few days and I've managed to successfully implement the feature to download playlists from erocast.me. Shall I create a new PR for this feature as the original author doesn't seem to be working on this anymore like Simon818 remarked? While creating a new PR I can also add the suggestions mentioned above by dirkf so it can all be in one neat package and we can close this PR for good.

Thoughts? @Simon818 @dirkf @RonniCGN
author: aaron-tan

body: @aaron-tan That's awesome. I think you should go ahead and submit it, particularly if it's already working on both playlists and individual tracks. We have no idea when the original PR author is going to respond and you have something that works today.
author: Simon818

body: I also would wish to have a list of extractors that have been removed due to the site being shut down. I will plan on slowly but surely, porting every feature from that branch to the main youtube-dl, or someone else that has the git program can, as I do not have access to the command line at this time.

author: nicolaasjan

body:
To those suggesting the malware extension should still be used: This is like playing with fire!!

The `Get cookies.txt` extension has been [exposed](https://www.reddit.com/r/youtubedl/comments/11i5vyq/psa_the_get_cookiestxt_extension_is_now_actively/) as MALWARE. This means a hacker is actively using it to steal your login details.

The only solution is to STOP USING THAT EXTENSION IMMEDIATELY and Change Your Passwords.
The extension cannot be trusted even a little bit! Do not use the extension with different settings. It is not safe.

Also, the functionality it provided is not unique, there are several other extensions that do the same job, and can be trusted (for now).
author: willsheppard

> > The only solution is to STOP USING THAT EXTENSION IMMEDIATELY and Change Your Passwords.
> 
> There is advice on what to do in the reddit post. Changing your passwords is not the advice, and could be counter productive (I'm not an expert)

By the way, even though this has now been merged, as the author of that post, I need to respond to this.

Changing your passwords is, in fact, part of the advice that I'm suggesting as doing so can cause some sites to invalidate login cookies.

The part that I say is counter-productive is clearing your cookies, not changing your passwords.

author: Rygle

body:
> > Code seems to download episodes OK, but not their subtitles
> 
> Same, no subtitles on several items I checked using yt-dlp, which is essentially this code afaik.

I wouldn't expect any further action at yt-dlp given that PR has landed and the related issue closed; the issues with UTF-16 encoding, <span> based formatting (as well as delivery of WebVTT subs when dxfp is requested in some cases*) makes further efforts around SBS subs unlikely, unless and until SBS themselves fix some stuff, despite comments over on Whirlpool. I think everyone's hair is quite safe here.

Suggest opening a new issue on the subs problems specifically, particularly if you don't want to delay this PR from landing in the short term and getting the basic downloading functions back. The bottom line is, as far as I know, the issues with subtitles are not related to anything in this PR, so no reason to delay it.

*for example, episodes 3 to 6 of 'Rogue Heroes'. If `--convert-sub` is used, a zero-length file results, because the input format to the conversion is unexpected.

```py
        'skip': 'Only available in Australia',
```
in the test cases, run the tests and post the results.
author: dirkf

body:
The problem is that the setting has been as it is for so long that if the default is changed people's workflows may be affected. The solution was already provided [10 years ago](https://github.com/ytdl-org/youtube-dl/issues/1709#issuecomment-27641159), ie `--no-mtime`.

yt-dlp also retains this default, presumably for the same reason.
author: dirkf

body:
a bit of pedantry that's necessary in my view: `--no-mtime` is not a solution to the problem "youtube-dl's default behavior is surprising and unconventional". that flag shouldn't be necessary for youtube-dl to behave in a reasonable way.

yt-dlp retains this default for no other reason than youtube-dl retaining it. see also: https://github.com/yt-dlp/yt-dlp/pull/6585#issuecomment-1477813545

is this your final decision?
author: flisk

body:
I don't think it's obvious that 
> ...default behavior is surprising

Personally I'm happy to see a downloaded file take on a modification time that matches the content. If you want to know when a file was put on the disk, use its creation time.

Also: https://github.com/yt-dlp/yt-dlp/pull/6585#issuecomment-1483808528
author: dirkf

body:
there is no "creation time" on unix-like systems. you may be confusing "ctime" with "creation time". the latter is only used as such on windows. https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/MAC_times#Change_time_and_creation_time_(ctime)

edit: apparently there is at least a non-standard "birth time" attribute on linux and macos.
author: flisk

body:
True enough, but dependent on filesystem: this just shows that most of my media collection is on NTFS external disks.

The WP article reflects exactly the issue being discussed in #1709.
author: dirkf

body:
>I don't think it's obvious that

linux documents `st_mtime` in `stat(2)` as such:
>This is the time of last modification of file data.

`stat(2)` on macos documents `st_mtime` as such:
>Time when file data last modified.  Changed by the mknod(2), utimes(2) and write(2) system calls.

since this is the expected default behavior for `st_mtime`, i don't think it's reasonable to deviate from it unless the user has explicitly asked for that.

>If you want to know when a file was put on the disk, use its creation time.

`mtime` is not the same as creation time, it's for recording when the data of a file was last modified. the fact that `mtime` matches the time of file creation if the file hasn't been modified after its creation is incidental.
author: flisk

body:
The controversial point is whether the "time of last modification of file data" (and the equivalent text for macOS) means when the current bits were written to that file, or when the file data that was written was modified.

The latter interpretation avoids the need for filesystem tools to parse file content or extended attributes to determine the "content modification time".
author: dirkf

body:
there clearly isn't a single correct interpretation of what this attribute should be used for, and changing youtube-dl's default behavior now to align with a different interpretation seems like a lateral move to me.

thanks for taking the time to discuss this.

body: You have successfully added a new CodeQL configuration `/language:python`. As part of the setup process, we have scanned this repository and found 23 existing alerts. Please check the repository [Security tab](/ytdl-org/youtube-dl/security/code-scanning?query=pr%3A31952+tool%3ACodeQL+is%3Aopen) to see all alerts.
author: github-advanced-security

body:
[The results of the test run](https://github.com/ytdl-org/youtube-dl/security/code-scanning?query=pr%3A31952+tool%3ACodeQL+is%3Aopen) don't offer anything much. The diagnostics highlight these features:
* "Inefficient regular expressions" which have never been a problem but might be adjusted in future if anyone cares
* "Incomplete URL substring sanitization": a deliberate code pattern that has no security impact in yt-dl (not that anything much does)
* "Default version of SSL/TLS may be insecure": invoked only on old Python versions that don't support more recent security protocols
* "Use of insecure SSL/TLS version": ditto
* "Use of a broken or weak cryptographic hashing algorithm on sensitive data": matching what's done by a target website, or locally with no security impact.

If the PR is correctly completed we could have this as an optional workflow to run every so often but it's not suitable to be incorporated into the build or test processes.

author: bartbroere
dirkf

body:
I do think we should make sure not to drop support for url's with the video id in there.
Url's like https://www.zapp.nl/programmas/de-bzt-show/gemist/KN_1687547 or https://npo.nl/npo3/de-braboneger-basht-jaaroverzicht/29-12-2017/POMS_AT_12271940
It also internally is how the NPO id system works (POMS).

My suggestion for a universal approach would be
- Extract POMS id from url with various implementations per url format/domain
- They all call the same function that take a POMS id and get's the url's
- Optionally enrich it with title and such if not already extracted from url
  - We could use the POMS api for this, but the url signing/api key is a little annoying even although those keys are public, `https://rs.poms.omroep.nl/v1/api/media/[id]` [example_response_POMS_NTR_388772.json](https://github.com/ytdl-org/youtube-dl/files/14463673/example_response_POMS_NTR_388772.json)
author: rvsit

body:
> My suggestion for a universal approach would be
> 
> * Extract POMS id from url with various implementations per url format/domain
> * They all call the same function that take a POMS id and get's the url's
> * Optionally enrich it with title and such if not already extracted from url
>   
>   * We could use the POMS api for this, but the url signing/api key is a little annoying even although those keys are public, `https://rs.poms.omroep.nl/v1/api/media/[id]` [example_response_POMS_NTR_388772.json](https://github.com/ytdl-org/youtube-dl/files/14463673/example_response_POMS_NTR_388772.json)

This indeed seems the best way to do this. I'll rework the code to fit in this pattern.

It successfully downloads all test URLs right now, but I'm not sure what it should say in the `ext` field in the `info_dict` of each test. I tried debugging it, but that gets tricky with the `format_selector` method being created at runtime.

The example URLs all download successfully with this command
```bash
python3.11 -m youtube_dl https://npo.nl/start/serie/vpro-tegenlicht/seizoen-11/zwart-geld-de-toekomst-komt-uit-afrika https://npo.nl/start/serie/zembla/seizoen-2015/wie-is-de-mol-2/ https://www.bnnvara.nl/videos/27455 https://ongehoordnederland.tv/2024/03/01/korte-clips/heeft-preppen-zin-betwijfel-dat-je-daar-echt-iets-aan-zult-hebben-bij-oorlog-lydia-daniel/ https://www.zapp.nl/programmas/zappsport/gemist/POMS_AT_811523 https://schooltv.nl/item/zapp-music-challenge-2015-zapp-music-challenge-2015 https://hetklokhuis.nl/dossier/142/zoek-het-uit/tv-uitzending/2987/aliens https://www.vpro.nl/programmas/tegenlicht/kijk/afleveringen/2015-2016/offline-als-luxe.html https://anderetijden.nl/programma/1/Andere-Tijden/aflevering/676/Duitse-soldaten-over-de-Slag-bij-Arnhem
```

but I can't get the test framework to pass. That gives me the error:
```
youtube_dl.utils.DownloadError: ERROR: requested format not available
```

So even with the failing tests I think this is an improvement over the current `master`, which no longer successfully downloads any NPO video. If anyone knows what it should say in `ext`, I can change it to the correct value.

There is an open PR there as well, let's start by tagging them so we can compare our work later: https://github.com/yt-dlp/yt-dlp/pull/11227
author: bartbroere

body:
> btw, related question. Is all content uploaded nowadays drm protected? This is a great PR, but it seems like it can only be used on older content as the rest is drm protected.

Yes, unfortunately it seems practically all new content is now DRM protected.

I kind of understand adding DRM when a broadcaster buys the rights to an international television show, but for things like the news I think it is a bit much (especially for a publicly funded organisation). In my opinion there's lots of legitimate reasons to want to use a news fragment offline.

And you're also correct on the older content still being downloadable. I think this older content makes it worth the effort to fix the NPO extractor here.

hmmmm. tested from NL. turns out https://vod.tvp.pl/filmy-dokumentalne,163/krzysztof-krawczyk--cale-moje-zycie,332512 plays in browser if logged in to a **free** account. the geoblock error message I'm getting when not logged in is this:
>Z powodów licencyjnych materiał jest niedostępny w Twoim kraju.
>
>Przepisy UE pozwalają abonentom, którzy w kraju zamieszkania (w tym przypadku w PL) zakupili pakiet TVP + utrzymać dostęp do materiałów wchodzących w skład pakietu, w czasie wizyty w innym kraju UE.

so I guess they wanted to implement this but someone made an assumption that an account logged in means SVOD and not logged in means AVOD? login is by cookies so `--cookies-from-browser firefox` worked for me on yt-dlp master

body: that's on embed (tvplayer2 api), this workaround is specific to VOD playlist api
author: selfisekai

body:
When I run that URL in the current PR, it's handled by `TVPVODVideoIE` and then handed off to `TVPEmbedIE` using the `externalUid`, where the geo-block is detected.

The data from the VOD API includes these values:
```
...
'countries': [{'id': 92, 'key': 'polska', 'name': 'Polska'}],
...
'geoipManualLock': False,
...
'loginRequired': False,
...
```
The test case https://vod.tvp.pl/website/krzysztof-krawczyk-cale-moje-zycie,51374466 is redirected via `TVPIE` to `332512` with the same result.

author: dirkf

body:
>`### video.toggle.sg seems deprecated`

As I understand it, the only supported toggle.sg URLs are video.toggle.sg/... which are now automatically redirected in the browser to mewatch.sg/... with the identical path component, even though the path structures for the domains are different, giving 404.

The new API that you are using here is geo-restricted, so I can't easily check whether a test URL like http://video.toggle.sg/en/clips/seraph-sun-aloysius-will-suddenly-sing-some-old-songs-in-high-pitch-on-set/343331 is correctly handled (as opposed to the equivalent new URL https://www.mewatch.sg/clips/Seraph-Sun-Aloysius-will-suddenly-sing-some-old-songs-in-high-pitch-on-set-84901 -- note capitalisation and `/343331` vs `-84901`).

If such video.toggle.sg URLs are no longer valid in-region, `ToggleIE` can be marked as not working or actually deleted. Otherwise it should be kept but only used for those URLs. In either case the URL pattern for `MeWatchIE` should be adapted to cover those URLs previously supported by `ToggleIE`.

The API isn't required for mewatch.sg because the video page, which is not generally geo-restricted, contains the IDs for the Kaltura hoster (which is also not generally geo-restricted). I have a working extractor that uses this information.
author: dirkf

body:
Hi @dirkf,

url_old: /343331 
url_new: -84901 

so I am getting the api for url_new is geo-restircted? what do you actually mean by this? Cuz the url_old returned a 404 for me, that's why I focused on the url_new.
author: shouldsee

body:
Sure. if the old URLs are 404 in-region, we should move everything into `MeWatchIE`. I could update your PR with my code that has your tests merged, or just attach it for you to adapt: how would you like to do it?

Incidentally the old video ID (like 343331) is still available as the `customId` of the item in the hydration JSON from the page. Depending on whether people need to match against the old ID (to avoid duplicate downloads), we could continue to use that rather than the obvious ID (like 84901). yt-dlp has the `old_archive_ids` system to handle this issue, but yt-dl doesn't. There is also the Kaltura ID, made up of the partner ID (seems always to be 2082311, but extractable from the JSON) and the Kaltura media ID (0_9s281rya, ditto). Unfortunately we only have two recognised ID fields to convey these three IDs (unless `episode_id` is misused).
author: dirkf

body:
> Kaltura ID

Hi dirkf, that's indeed important consideration with de-duplication. I am happy for you to merge your code in because I am not actively working on this. The objective was to re-enable downloading from the mewatch.sg site, and I am happy as long as the url can be parsed and downloaded.

BTW, are you implying the old urls being 404 is a region-specific thing? I don't have good vpns at the moment so not 100% sure.
author: shouldsee

body:
Hi @shouldsee, are you still working on this PR? If not, I am happy to work on this extractor and try to have it pushed downstream to yt-dlp too, if possible.

I have also poked around mewatch.sg and found out that apart from the hydration JSON on the webpage itself and the `/videos` endpoint of the new API, there is also a `/page` endpoint that gives the details of a `/show` (e.g. <https://www.mewatch.sg/show/Emerald-Hill-The-Little-Nyonya-Story-524116>), especially a list of available episodes so we may be able to download all of them in a batch, similar to how a YouTube playlist is downloaded (see snippet below)?

```
>>> import requests, json

> [craftpag commented Mar 17, 2024](https://github.com/ytdl-org/youtube-dl/issues/14960#issuecomment-2002651220):
>

> > Playlist: http://www.dplay.no/neste-sommer/
> > A while ago, do you have a new solution to download neste sommer and praktisk info med jon almaas from discoveryplus.com/no with youtube-dl?
> > Hope you can help with a solution:)

I don't have a subscription there anymore, but there are some free videos available on the new site. However, trying to download those tells me it's only available for registered users even though they are free. Seems like you need to add the `st` cookie to download them still, ~~but I don't know how it creates that cookie.~~ Ah, seems like you just have to make a regular GET request to `https://eu1-prod-direct.discoveryplus.com/token?realm=dplay&shortlived=true` where it is the `["data"]["attributes"]["token"]` value in the json response.
https://www.discoveryplus.com/no/video/alle-mot-alle/trailer-sesong-8
When I try to manually download the mpd manifest with yt-dlp it tells me the video is drm protected. But it seems like it's possible to bypass that by setting `"drmSupported": false,` in the `https://eu1-prod-direct.discoveryplus.com/playback/v3/videoPlaybackInfo` json request so it downloads the manifest url with `dash_clear_fmp4` instead of `dash_widevinePlayready_fmp4`.
author: MrDemocracy

body:
some notes from https://github.com/yt-dlp/yt-dlp/commit/39e6c4cb44b9292e89ac0afec3cd0afc2ae8775f :
- you'll need the changes to `DiscoveryPlusBaseIE._download_video_playback_info` from https://github.com/yt-dlp/yt-dlp/commit/90d2da311bbb5dc06f385ee428c7e4590936e995
- for IEs subclassed from DiscoveryPlusBaseIE, `siteLookupKey` is now required in `x-disco-params` (it's `_PRODUCT`, and DiscoveryPlusItaly's should be `dplus_it`)
- also for IEs subclasses from DiscoveryPlusBaseIE, the `x-disco-client` is always `f'WEB:UNKNOWN:dplus_us:{self._DISCO_CLIENT_VER}'`; note the `dplus_us` instead of `{self._PRODUCT}`. This remains true even for non-US sites
- `discovery.py` and `discoverygo.py` are irrelevant now and can be removed
- `DIYNetworkIE`, `MotorTrendIE`, `MotorTrendOnDemandIE`, `GlobalCyclingNetworkPlusIE` are dead and can be removed
author: bashonly

body: An [issue](https://github.com/ytdl-org/youtube-dl/issues/7201) was opened previously for this site and subsequently closed as it was determined that this site hosts copyrighted content. https://github.com/ytdl-org/youtube-dl/issues/7201
author: JChris246

body:
Since content is generally subject to copyright, that's not the deciding factor. Look at other sites supported by yt-dl and you will find copyrighted material that may be hosted with, or (shockingly) without, the copyright holder's permission: not just porn, but user-generated content sites like YouTube.

Also, #7201 applies to the site as it was in 2015, though there's no indication of whether the site has changed.

The project's policy has two parts:
>Make sure that site you are requesting is not dedicated to copyright infringement, see https://yt-dl.org/copyright-infringement. youtube-dl does not support such sites. 

>In order for site support request to be accepted all provided example URLs should not violate any copyrights.

The linked guidance is this:
>As a matter of policy (as well as legality), youtube-dl does not include support for services that specialize in infringing copyright. As a rule of thumb, if you cannot easily find a video that the service is quite obviously allowed to distribute (i.e. that has been uploaded by the creator, the creator's distributor, or is published under a free license), the service is probably unfit for inclusion to youtube-dl.
>
>A note on the service that they don't host the infringing content, but just link to those who do, is evidence that the service should not be included into youtube-dl. The same goes for any DMCA note when the whole front page of the service is filled with videos they are not allowed to distribute. A "fair use" note is equally unconvincing if the service shows copyright-protected videos in full without authorization.

I would say that "the whole front page of the service is filled with videos they are not allowed to distribute" could cover almost every porn site that yt-dl supports. I looked at a large sample of yt-dl[p] porn site extractors and most failed the test: eg, daft.sex.

The porn ecosystem is entirely different from, say, broadcasting and movie streaming sites. If a movie is available on BBC iPlayer, we can take it that the BBC is distributing it with permission. Sites like YouTube and Vimeo do distribute actual user-generated content alongside material for which the submitter may not have the rights. In fact, YT conspires with its users and the music industry to promote ad-supported content submitted by users who do not own the content.

A typical porn site provides no explicit copyright attribution. The user can't be expected to know which video might have been licensed by the copyright owner, even in the presence of a logo or watermark. Does the site have some sort of ad-revenue-sharing deal with content owners? Is the content being supplied legitimately as a promotion? No-one can tell for sure, and the waters are further muddied because the same company may operate both a paid content site and ad-supported "user-generated" sites that include old content from the paid site.

My interpretation of the policy is rather that yt-dl is like a web browser and it cannot be responsible for identifying whether any particular content is legitimate. If a user is concerned about some material, don't download it. The site must allow copyright owners to remove unauthorised content. If the site claims to follow DMCA, that should be enough, as it is for YouTube. Therefore, I don't entirely support the statement about DMCA in the guidance quoted above.

Also, the project requirement "example URLs should not violate any copyrights" seems to be very difficult to interpret in this context. One might think that a URL is a reference that cannot be restricted by copyright: otherwise no-one could write `the movie "Top Gun - Maverick"` without permission. If the policy is saying that no example URL should reference content that is explicitly being distributed without permission ("hey l33t guys, grab this warez movie I hacked"), that's clear enough. But anything more than that fails in the UGC or porn site context.

Regarding HQPorner, there are particular issues:
1. it claims to host user-submitted content, but there's no identification of the uploader (the site says it registers users manually)
2. its terms require that users have permission to post videos, but all videos that I checked appeared to be unedited commercial productions
3. its terms acknowledge DMCA and there is a dedicated email address for DMCA take-down requests, but DMCA is linked in the terms and contact pages, rather than on the main video and list pages.

Because of point 1 above, as well as the ecosystem context, it's difficult to tell whether any video is posted with valid permission. Maybe (point 2) the material is actually being provided by the owners as a sort of ad-supported front? The example URL in https://github.com/yt-dlp/yt-dlp/pull/7147 showed a gigantic banner ad for the apparent owner of the video.

In this PR I selected a more anonymous video for the main test.

author: fmonts
fmonts

body:
I'm not convinced about this.

The existing logic tries to avoid re-encoding the audio unless the target container requires it. As I see it, this change on its own will generally result in worse audio quality than what the site offered: if you ask for lower quality you'll get it and if you ask for higher quality you'll get a degraded version of the original with a higher nominal bitrate. Unless you always want to dumb down the MP3 quality to reduce filesize, that doesn't sound good.

You could try this
```diff
-            elif filecodec in ['aac', 'flac', 'mp3', 'vorbis', 'opus']:
+            elif filecodec in ['aac', 'flac', 'vorbis', 'opus'] or (filecodec == 'mp3' and self._preferredquality is None):
```
If you specify `--audio-quality ...`, that should be passed into `self._preferredquality` and cause the re-encoding that you want to occur. A more complex change could extend the same processing to the other audio formats.

The FFMPEG audio option processing is definitely a target for updates. _yt-dlp_ has some more sophisticated processing but I haven't studied it fully.
author: dirkf

body:
I see. I've amended the commit to incorporate your suggestion which I feel achieves both aims...

> if you ask for lower quality you'll get it
which satisfies my need to ensure a standardized bit rate

> and [preventing asking] for higher quality [and getting] a degraded version of the original with a higher nominal bitrate
which avoids the needless and unexpected audio degradation
